title_en: Japanese Grandma's Candied Ginger in Soy (Shoga Tsukudani)
meta_description_en: Traditional Japanese preserved ginger recipe from Nagano. A grandma's pantry staple simmered in soy sauce, sugar, and kombu — perfect over rice.
intro_en: In the cold mountain region of Nagano, preserving food for winter has always been essential. This tsukudani — ginger slowly simmered in a sweet soy glaze — is a beloved pantry staple that Japanese grandmothers make in large batches each summer when young ginger is in season.
story_en: Nagano's cold climate gave rise to a rich culture of preserved foods. Tsukudani has long been a year-round pantry staple, and many grandmothers would make large batches when fresh young ginger appeared in stores. Adding Fujikko (a commercial kombu tsukudani paste) gives this version extra umami and a sticky richness that sets it apart.
cultural_background_en: Shinshu (Nagano) is known for its harsh winters and deep tradition of preserved foods. Tsukudani — ingredients simmered down in soy sauce and sugar until glossy — is a quintessential Japanese pantry item.
episode_en: When young ginger appeared in stores each summer, grandma would put on her backpack and walk to Tsuruya, a local supermarket about 15 km round trip — an all-day journey. She always insisted that only Tsuruya's ginger could make proper tsukudani. She passed away three years ago, but her handwritten recipe notes are still carefully preserved by the family.

items.name: Fresh young ginger (shin-shoga)
items.grandma_amount: A generous full bag (enough to fill the pot)

items.name: Vinegar (for parboiling)
items.grandma_amount: Just a splash in the water

steps_en.text: Slice the fresh young ginger thinly.
Parboil the ginger in water with vinegar.
steps_en.tip: This removes bitterness and mellows the spicy heat

steps_en.text: Rinse in cold water and drain well.
Combine simmering liquid ingredients and simmer the ginger.
When the liquid reduces, stir in the Fujikko kombu paste.
Sprinkle with sesame seeds and it's done.
steps_en.tip: Keeps in the fridge for about one week

source_types_en: Based on handwritten notes left by grandma herself

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Homemade Ohagi (Sweet Rice Balls with Red Bean)
meta_description_en: Authentic Kyoto-style ohagi — sweet mochi rice balls wrapped in homemade red bean paste. Traditional Japanese dessert for seasonal celebrations.
intro_en: Ohagi are beloved Japanese sweets made of sticky rice wrapped in sweet red bean paste, traditionally prepared during equinox festivals. In Kyoto, grandmothers make these by hand for generations — each family with its own secret ratio of sugar to beans.
story_en: Ohagi is a classic Japanese sweet eaten during spring and autumn equinox celebrations. The same sweet changes its name with the seasons — 'ohagi' in autumn after the bush clover, and 'botamochi' in spring after the peony. In Kyoto, chunky red bean paste is preferred.
cultural_background_en: Ohagi is one of Japan's most iconic homemade sweets, deeply tied to Buddhist equinox traditions. Each grandmother has her own unique touch on sweetness and texture.
episode_en: Every visit to grandma's home in Kyoto, she would make ohagi for the family. Growing up with her handmade ohagi shaped my lifelong love of Japanese sweets. Even now, I've never found anything that tastes as good.

steps_en.text: Wash both rices together, soak 30 min, then cook in rice cooker.
steps_en.tip: Soaking ensures even cooking

steps_en.text: Simmer azuki beans until soft, fold in sugar and salt to make paste.
steps_en.tip: Stir gently for chunky texture

steps_en.text: Partially mash cooked rice and shape into balls.
steps_en.tip: Leave some grain texture

steps_en.text: Wrap rice balls in red bean paste using plastic wrap.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Octopus and Seaweed in Sweet Miso (Tako Sumiso)
meta_description_en: Traditional Osaka tako sumiso — octopus and wakame in sweet miso vinaigrette. A lucky dish served at Japanese celebrations and birthdays.
intro_en: In the Kansai region, octopus is considered lucky because 'tako' sounds like 'much happiness.' This elegant dish of octopus and wakame in sweet vinegar-miso dressing has been a birthday staple in Osaka families for generations.
story_en: In Kansai, octopus is cherished as a lucky ingredient — 'tako' can mean 'much happiness.' This vinegar-miso dressed salad is a beloved classic enjoyed by all ages.
cultural_background_en: Octopus holds special cultural significance in western Japan, where it's associated with good fortune and celebrations.
episode_en: My grandmother always made this for my birthday since octopus is considered lucky. Now I make it for my daughter's birthday every year. The sweet-tangy balance is always a hit.

steps_en.text: Cut octopus into bite-sized pieces.
Rehydrate dried wakame.
steps_en.tip: Quick soak only — too long makes it watery

steps_en.text: Mix dressing: miso, vinegar, sugar, and mentsuyu.
steps_en.tip: Balance sweetness and tanginess

steps_en.text: Toss octopus and wakame with dressing.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Whole Sea Bream Rice (Taimeshi)
meta_description_en: Authentic Ehime taimeshi — whole sea bream cooked with rice in a clay pot. Traditional Japanese celebration dish passed down through generations.
intro_en: Taimeshi is one of Japan's most celebrated rice dishes from Ehime Prefecture. A whole sea bream is grilled then cooked with rice in a clay pot, infusing every grain with delicate flavor. This Matsuyama-style version is reserved for life's most special moments.
story_en: Along the Seto Inland Sea, sea bream fishing has thrived for centuries. Ehime has two styles: Matsuyama-style with whole fish cooked in a clay pot, and Uwajima-style with fresh sashimi over rice. This is the Matsuyama tradition.
cultural_background_en: Sea bream (tai) is Japan's quintessential celebration fish — 'medetai' (auspicious) contains 'tai,' cementing its place at weddings and milestones.
episode_en: On special days like birthdays, grandma would make her delicious taimeshi. The gentle flavor of the whole sea bream cooked into the rice is forever linked with my childhood memories.

steps_en.text: Clean the sea bream, pat dry, salt both sides, rest 20 minutes.
steps_en.tip: Salting draws out fishy odors and concentrates umami

steps_en.text: Grill until the surface is nicely charred.
steps_en.tip: Toasted flavor infuses into the rice

steps_en.text: Place rice, seasonings, and dashi in clay pot. Lay fish, ginger, and kombu on top. Cook.
Remove fish, flake the meat, fold back into rice with mitsuba.
steps_en.tip: Watch for small bones while flaking

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Kombu Everything Stew (Gottani)
meta_description_en: Traditional Toyama gottani stew — hearty kombu and root vegetables embodying Japan's 'mottainai' zero-waste philosophy. A warming grandma's recipe.
intro_en: This rustic stew from Toyama is the ultimate 'use what you have' dish. True to the 'mottainai' spirit, grandmothers never throw away kombu after making dashi — they tie it into knots and simmer it right into the stew alongside whatever vegetables are on hand.
story_en: Toyama Prefecture has been a major port for trading ships that brought kombu from Hokkaido, giving the region one of Japan's highest kelp consumption rates. The 'mottainai' waste-nothing spirit means dashi kombu is never discarded but repurposed as an ingredient.
cultural_background_en: Toyama boasts some of Japan's highest kombu consumption thanks to historical trade routes. The mottainai philosophy means kombu used for dashi is always repurposed rather than discarded.
episode_en: Grandma would say 'Nothing should go to waste' as she transformed fridge leftovers into this magical stew. On cold winter nights, listening to the pot bubbling while the family chatted — those are memories I'll never forget.

items.name: Kombu (dried kelp)
items.note: Reuse from making dashi — tie into knots or slice

steps_en.text: Peel and cut daikon, carrot, and potatoes into chunks.
Slice shiitake and konnyaku. Cut chicken into pieces.
Pour kombu dashi into pot, add vegetables and chicken.
steps_en.tip: Don't discard the dashi kombu — add it in too

steps_en.text: Skim foam, add soy sauce, mirin, and sugar.
Simmer until tender. Adjust salt at end.
steps_en.tip: The longer you simmer, the more umami develops

grandma_notes_en: Never waste anything. Use every bit of the kombu.
Throw in whatever's in the fridge — that's the secret to making it delicious.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Wakayama-Style Fried Rice (Yakimeshi)
meta_description_en: Homestyle Japanese fried rice from Wakayama topped with delicate egg ribbons. Simple, satisfying grandma's everyday comfort food.
intro_en: Unlike Chinese-style chahan, this Wakayama yakimeshi is a gentler take on fried rice. Topped with thin egg ribbons and seasoned simply with soy sauce, it's everyday comfort food that Japanese families have been making for generations.
story_en: Yakimeshi differs from Chinese-style chahan in its gentler seasoning and home-style presentation. This version tops the rice with delicate kinshi tamago (thin egg ribbons).
cultural_background_en: While 'chahan' refers to Chinese-style fried rice, 'yakimeshi' is the more casual Japanese term for homestyle fried rice — simpler and more lightly seasoned than restaurant versions.
episode_en: This was grandma's quick everyday dinner. The delicate egg ribbons on top were her special touch — she said they made even simple fried rice feel like a proper meal.

steps_en.text: Make egg ribbons: cook a thin omelet, roll and slice thinly.
Stir-fry rice in hot oil.
Season with soy sauce, salt, pepper. Add green onion.
Top with egg ribbons and serve.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Gameni Chicken Stew (Chikuzenni)
meta_description_en: Authentic gameni from Fukuoka — hearty chicken and root vegetable stew. Known as chikuzenni nationwide, this is an essential Japanese New Year's dish.
intro_en: Gameni is Fukuoka's signature stew, known nationwide as chikuzenni. What sets it apart is that ingredients are stir-fried before braising — locking in flavor and creating a rich depth. Every Japanese grandmother has her own version, and it's a must at New Year's.
story_en: The name 'gameni' comes from Hakata dialect meaning 'to gather everything together.' Chicken, burdock, lotus root, taro, and konnyaku are stir-fried first then slowly simmered.
cultural_background_en: Gameni is one of Japan's most iconic home-cooked dishes. The stir-fry-before-simmer technique creates a toasted depth of flavor that makes it a fixture of osechi New Year cuisine.
episode_en: Every New Year's, grandma spent hours making gameni — carefully cutting each vegetable into decorative shapes. She said the secret was patience: never rush the stir-frying step.

steps_en.text: Heat oil, stir-fry chicken until browned.
steps_en.tip: This stir-fry step is what makes gameni special

steps_en.text: Add hard vegetables and continue stir-frying.
Add taro, konnyaku, shiitake. Pour in dashi.
Add sake, sugar, mirin. Simmer.
Add soy sauce, simmer until glazed.
steps_en.tip: Vegetables should be tender and coated

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Sweet Potato Mochi (Imo Mochi)
meta_description_en: Easy Japanese sweet potato mochi — soft, chewy snacks from mashed sweet potato. A traditional grandma's treat loved by kids and adults.
intro_en: These sweet, chewy cakes made from mashed sweet potato and starch are a beloved Japanese snack that grandmothers make for generations. Pan-fried in butter until golden, they're irresistible.
story_en: Imo mochi is a traditional autumn snack where mashed sweet potato is mixed with starch to create soft, chewy cakes. A favorite after-school or teatime treat.
cultural_background_en: Sweet potato mochi represents the Japanese tradition of creating treats from humble ingredients, showcasing the natural sweetness of Japanese sweet potatoes.
episode_en: Grandma made these every autumn. She let us kids help shape them, and we watched impatiently as they turned golden in the pan.

steps_en.text: Steam or boil sweet potato until soft.
Mash while hot, mix in sugar and starch.
steps_en.tip: Starch gives the chewy mochi texture

steps_en.text: Shape into flat rounds.
Pan-fry in butter until golden.
steps_en.tip: Medium-low heat for crispy outside, soft center

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Pork-Wrapped Asparagus and Cheese Fry
meta_description_en: Crispy Japanese pork rolls with asparagus and melty cheese — a beloved grandma's recipe from Gifu. Golden fried comfort food at its best.
intro_en: Thin pork slices wrapped around asparagus and melty cheese, breaded and deep-fried — this is the indulgent home cooking that Japanese grandmothers make to spoil the family.
story_en: This dish combines Western ingredients with Japanese panko frying technique. The contrast of crispy coating, savory pork, crunchy asparagus, and melting cheese makes it a family favorite.
cultural_background_en: Japanese home cooking blends Western ingredients with traditional techniques. Panko breadcrumbs create an exceptionally crispy, light coating. Wrapping vegetables in pork is called 'nikumaki.'
episode_en: This was grandma's special treat for celebrations. She carefully wrapped each roll to prevent cheese from leaking. Biting through the crispy coating to find melty cheese was pure joy.

steps_en.text: Wrap asparagus and cheese in pork slices. Roll tightly.
steps_en.tip: Wrap firmly so cheese doesn't escape

steps_en.text: Coat in flour, egg, then panko.
Deep-fry at 170-180°C until golden.
steps_en.tip: Fry in batches for even cooking

steps_en.text: Drain and serve hot.
steps_en.tip: Best while cheese is still melty

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Chicken and Chestnut Stew (Torikuri)
meta_description_en: Hokkaido's torikuri — tender chicken simmered with chestnuts in savory-sweet sauce. A hearty Japanese grandma's autumn comfort dish from the north.
intro_en: Torikuri is a warming Hokkaido stew bringing together tender chicken and sweet chestnuts in rich broth. Dating to Hokkaido's frontier days, it makes the most of autumn's harvest.
story_en: Torikuri — 'chicken and chestnuts' — is a traditional Hokkaido dish from the frontier era. The combination of protein-rich chicken and naturally sweet chestnuts creates a deeply satisfying dish for cold northern nights.
cultural_background_en: Hokkaido's food culture was shaped by settlers who adapted traditions to the island's harsh climate. Torikuri reflects this pioneering spirit — simple, nourishing, designed for long winters.
episode_en: Every autumn, grandma spent an afternoon peeling chestnuts by hand to make this stew. She said the work was worth it because nothing else tasted like freshly peeled chestnuts simmered with chicken.

steps_en.text: Brown the chicken pieces in a pot.
Add chestnuts and dashi. Bring to a simmer.
Add sake, sugar, mirin. Simmer gently.
Add soy sauce, simmer until chestnuts are tender and liquid reduces to a glaze.
steps_en.tip: Low and slow — rush it and chestnuts fall apart

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Sand Lance (Ikanago no Kugini)
meta_description_en: Traditional Hyogo kugini — tiny sand lance fish simmered in sweet soy until nail-shaped. A beloved spring pantry staple from Kobe grandmothers.
intro_en: Every spring along Hyogo's coast, grandmothers make enormous batches of kugini — tiny fish simmered in sweet soy until they curl like nails. Families pack it into jars to share with relatives across Japan.
story_en: Ikanago no kugini is one of the most beloved spring traditions in coastal Hyogo. The name 'kugini' (nail simmering) comes from how the tiny fish curl during cooking. Families jar it and send to relatives as a taste of spring.
cultural_background_en: Sand lance season in early spring triggers a cooking frenzy across Hyogo's coastal communities. The tradition of mailing jars to distant family is a beloved springtime ritual.
episode_en: When spring came, grandma's kitchen smelled like sweet soy sauce for days. She'd fill glass jars and mail them across Japan — her way of staying connected with loved ones far away.

steps_en.text: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sugar in a flat pan. Bring to boil.
Spread fish evenly in the boiling sauce.
steps_en.tip: Don't stir — they'll break apart

steps_en.text: Add ginger. Simmer until fish curl like nails.
steps_en.tip: Shake the pan gently instead of stirring

steps_en.text: Spread on tray to cool. Store in clean jars.
steps_en.tip: Keeps weeks refrigerated

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Sweet and Spicy Ginger Rice Bowl (Shoga Amakara Don)
meta_description_en: Quick Japanese ginger rice bowl from Fukushima — bold sweet-savory flavors over steaming rice. A grandma's weeknight recipe with pantry staples.
intro_en: This punchy ginger rice bowl from Fukushima proves that Japanese home cooking doesn't need fancy ingredients. With just ginger, basic seasonings, and rice, grandmothers turn pantry staples into deeply satisfying meals.
story_en: This sweet-spicy ginger rice bowl is a Fukushima classic. Bold ginger flavor with sweet-savory sauce makes it irresistible over hot rice — no special ingredients needed.
cultural_background_en: Donburi (rice bowls) are the quintessential Japanese quick meal. This ginger version showcases how grandmothers create maximum flavor from minimal ingredients.
episode_en: This was grandma's go-to weeknight dinner. The whole house would fill with the aroma of ginger and soy sauce. Even now, sizzling ginger takes me right back to her kitchen.

steps_en.text: Julienne the ginger.
Stir-fry in sesame oil until fragrant.
Add soy sauce, mirin, sugar, sake. Simmer until thickened.
Serve over hot rice.
steps_en.tip: Top with sesame seeds or green onion

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Wild Garlic in Vinegar Miso (Nobiru Sumiso)
meta_description_en: Seasonal Japanese wild garlic dressed in sweet vinegar miso. A traditional spring foraging dish from Tokyo's countryside grandmothers.
intro_en: Nobiru is a wild plant that grows in fields around Tokyo. Foraging for it is a beloved spring tradition. The sharp, garlicky bulbs dressed in sweet vinegar-miso capture the essence of Japanese spring.
story_en: Wild garlic grows naturally in Tokyo's suburban fields and is one of the first signs of spring. Dressing it in vinegar-miso is the classic springtime preparation.
cultural_background_en: Japan has a deep tradition of sansai (wild mountain vegetable) cooking. Nobiru is one of the most accessible wild plants for foraging around Tokyo.
episode_en: Every spring, grandma took us to the fields to dig up nobiru. She knew exactly where to find them. We'd bring home a basketful and she'd serve them that evening — our family's way of welcoming spring.

steps_en.text: Clean nobiru, trim roots and damaged leaves.
Blanch briefly, plunge into ice water.
steps_en.tip: Quick blanching keeps the crunch

steps_en.text: Mix dressing: miso, vinegar, sugar, mustard.
Dress the nobiru and serve.
steps_en.tip: Best eaten same day

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Royal Fern with Fried Tofu (Zenmai no Nimono)
meta_description_en: Traditional simmered zenmai fern from Niigata. A mountain vegetable dish bringing the taste of spring to the table year-round. Authentic grandma recipe.
intro_en: In Niigata's mountain villages, spring means zenmai season. Dried and preserved, these wild ferns sustain families year-round. Simmered with fried tofu, this brings the deep, earthy flavor of the mountains to every meal.
story_en: Zenmai fern is one of the most prized wild mountain vegetables. Harvested in spring, dried in the sun, and stored for year-round use. Simmered with aburaage, it captures the mountain's essence.
cultural_background_en: Wild mountain vegetables are central to Niigata's mountain food culture. Drying zenmai allowed communities to enjoy spring flavors even during harsh winters.
episode_en: Grandma stored dried zenmai carefully, using it sparingly throughout the year. When she simmered it, the house filled with an earthy aroma that meant spring had arrived — even in winter.

steps_en.text: Rehydrate dried zenmai. Cut into pieces.
Cut aburaage into strips.
Simmer zenmai and aburaage in dashi with seasonings.
Continue until flavors absorb and liquid reduces.
steps_en.tip: Mountain vegetables need slow cooking for best flavor

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Kencho Stew (Yamaguchi-Style Daikon and Tofu)
meta_description_en: Traditional kencho from Yamaguchi — simmered daikon and crumbled tofu in savory broth. A humble, heartwarming Japanese grandma's everyday dish.
intro_en: Kencho is Yamaguchi's signature stew — thin daikon slices simmered with crumbled tofu in a simple savory broth. It's everyday comfort food that tastes even better the next day.
story_en: Thinly sliced daikon and crumbled tofu simmered in light soy broth — simple ingredients that come together into something greater than their parts.
cultural_background_en: Kencho represents the quiet, everyday food that sustains families — simple, nourishing, deeply satisfying. It reflects making the most of humble ingredients.
episode_en: Grandma made kencho so often it felt like it was always on the table. She always said the best kencho was made the day before and reheated.

steps_en.text: Heat sesame oil, stir-fry daikon and carrot.
Add crumbled tofu and aburaage. Stir-fry briefly.
Add dashi, bring to simmer.
Season with soy sauce and mirin. Simmer until daikon is translucent.
steps_en.tip: Tastes even better reheated the next day

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Yuzu-Scented Mixed Sushi with Red Beans (Kakimaze)
meta_description_en: Tokushima kakimaze — fragrant yuzu citrus mixed sushi with kintoki beans. A festive Japanese grandma's dish for celebrations and festivals.
intro_en: Kakimaze is Tokushima's festive mixed sushi infused with bright yuzu citrus and studded with sweet kintoki beans. Unlike standard chirashi sushi, kakimaze gets its distinctive character from yuzu juice mixed right into the rice.
story_en: Kakimaze features the refreshing fragrance of yuzu citrus — a Tokushima specialty. Kintoki beans and seasonal vegetables are mixed into rice seasoned with yuzu juice.
cultural_background_en: Tokushima is famous for yuzu citrus, and kakimaze showcases it beautifully. The addition of sweet kintoki beans makes this a uniquely Tokushima creation.
episode_en: At every family gathering, grandma prepared kakimaze. The kitchen smelled of yuzu as she squeezed fresh juice into the rice. She said yuzu was the soul of the dish — without it, it was just ordinary mixed sushi.

steps_en.text: Cook rice firm. Mix sushi vinegar with yuzu juice, fold into hot rice.
steps_en.tip: Use a cutting motion — don't mash

steps_en.text: Prepare toppings: simmer vegetables, cook beans, make egg ribbons.
Fold beans, carrot, shiitake into rice.
Garnish with egg ribbons.
steps_en.tip: Best at room temperature for maximum yuzu fragrance

title_en: Japanese Grandma's New Year's Mochi Soup (Ozoni - Kyoto Style)
meta_description_en: Traditional Kyoto-style ozoni — white miso soup with round mochi for Japanese New Year. The most important grandma recipe of the year.
intro_en: Ozoni is the quintessential Japanese New Year's soup. Kyoto's version features round mochi in rich white miso broth — sweeter and more elegant than eastern Japan's clear soy versions. Making ozoni on New Year's morning is one of a grandmother's most important traditions.
story_en: Ozoni is Japan's most important New Year dish. Kyoto's version uses round mochi in sweet white miso broth, reflecting the ancient capital's refined aesthetic. Regional variations make ozoni a window into Japan's diversity.
cultural_background_en: Every region has its own ozoni tradition — from Kyoto's white miso to Tokyo's clear soy broth. 'What's in your family's ozoni?' is a common New Year conversation topic.
episode_en: New Year's always started with grandma's ozoni. She'd get up before dawn to prepare the white miso broth. The sweet, rich aroma was the smell of New Year itself.

steps_en.text: Make kombu dashi. Simmer taro, daikon, carrot until tender.
Dissolve white miso into broth.
steps_en.tip: Don't boil after adding miso — it destroys the flavor

steps_en.text: Toast or boil the mochi until soft.
steps_en.tip: Toasting adds charred aroma

steps_en.text: Place mochi in bowls, ladle soup over, garnish with yuzu zest.
steps_en.tip: Yuzu adds festive fragrance

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Royal Fern (Zenmai no Nitsuke - Oita)
meta_description_en: Traditional Oita simmered zenmai fern with fried tofu. A preserved mountain vegetable dish from Japan's countryside grandmothers.
intro_en: In Oita's mountain villages, grandmothers preserve zenmai fern by sun-drying, creating a year-round pantry staple. Reconstituted and simmered with fried tofu, it brings the earthy taste of mountain foraging to everyday meals.
story_en: In Oita's mountain villages, spring means zenmai season. Dried fern shoots last throughout the year. Simmered with aburaage, they develop a rich, earthy depth perfect for rice or bento.
cultural_background_en: Mountain communities developed preservation techniques to extend brief foraging seasons. Dried zenmai carries spring's wild mountain flavors through the entire year.
episode_en: Every spring, grandma headed into the mountains for zenmai. She'd spend days drying it on bamboo racks. That dried zenmai lasted all year — appearing in simmered dishes, bento, and New Year cooking.

steps_en.text: Soak dried zenmai overnight. Cut into pieces.
Stir-fry zenmai and aburaage in sesame oil.
Add dashi and seasonings. Simmer over low heat.
Continue until liquid reduces and flavors absorb.
steps_en.tip: Mountain vegetables need long, slow cooking

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Tea Porridge (Chagayu)
meta_description_en: Learn how to make Chagayu, a traditional Japanese tea porridge from Wakayama. This comforting japanese recipe uses bancha or green tea for a light, aromatic home cooking classic.
intro_en: Chagayu is a soothing rice porridge simmered in Japanese tea, enjoyed as everyday comfort food in the Kinki region. This traditional japanese food is remarkably simple yet deeply satisfying, with the gentle aroma of tea infusing every spoonful.
story_en: Chagayu (tea porridge) has been a daily staple in Wakayama and Nara prefectures for generations. The porridge is cooked in bancha or green tea, giving it a subtle fragrance and a clean, refreshing flavor that pairs beautifully with pickles and grilled fish.
cultural_background_en: Tea porridge has been cherished as a regional specialty across the Kinki area, particularly in Wakayama and Nara. Affectionately called 'okaisan,' it was eaten at all meals throughout the day as an everyday household dish.
episode_en: The porridge my grandmother always made was chagayu. I loved eating it with pickles and grilled fish. I remember being so happy when it appeared at dinner. She used green tea instead of bancha, so it had a slightly different flavor, but it was always delicious.

items.name: Water
items.note: Use more water than you would for regular rice
items.grandma_amount: more than you'd use for regular rice

items.name: Leftover cooked rice (optional)
items.note: You can also use leftover rice

steps_en.text: Wash the rice, then add it (or leftover rice) to a pot of brewed tea. Use your preferred type of tea.
Simmer on low heat for 10 to 15 minutes until done.
steps_en.tip: Cooking slowly on low heat allows the tea flavor to deeply infuse the rice.

grandma_notes_en: Since it's porridge, use more water than you would for regular rice. The rest is up to your taste.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Nikujaga with Thick Fried Tofu (Family Recipe)
meta_description_en: A unique family-recipe Nikujaga from Chiba using thick fried tofu instead of shirataki noodles. This traditional japanese food features oyster sauce and butter as secret ingredients for rich home cooking.
intro_en: Nikujaga is Japan's quintessential comfort food, but every family has their own twist. This Chiba family's version swaps shirataki noodles for thick fried tofu (atsuage), with oyster sauce and butter as secret ingredients passed down through generations.
story_en: While nikujaga is a staple of Japanese home cooking, this family's version has a little secret. Instead of shirataki noodles, they use thick fried tofu, and the hidden flavors of oyster sauce and butter have been passed down for generations. This dish always appeared on the table during New Year's and Golden Week visits to the grandparents' home.
cultural_background_en: Nikujaga is said to have originated in the Meiji era as a Japanese adaptation of Western beef stew. Each family has their own unique touch with ingredients and seasoning. In this Chiba household, using thick fried tofu instead of shirataki noodles has been the tradition for generations.
episode_en: In our family, nikujaga never had shirataki noodles. When I asked my grandmother why, she explained that shirataki doesn't fill you up, so thick fried tofu is better because it's more substantial. Adding oyster sauce and butter as hidden flavors brings out the best in the tofu. That's why our family has always used thick fried tofu instead. It made perfect sense, and ever since then, nikujaga without thick fried tofu just feels incomplete.

items.name: Atsuage (thick fried tofu)
items.note: Used instead of shirataki noodles in this family recipe

items.name: Water
items.grandma_amount: enough to just cover the ingredients

steps_en.text: Cut the potatoes, carrots, and onions into bite-sized pieces. Cut the thick fried tofu into bite-sized pieces as well.
Stir-fry the pork belly in a pot until the color changes, then add the vegetables and stir briefly.
Add enough water to just cover the ingredients, then add soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and dashi granules.
Add the thick fried tofu, then the secret ingredients: oyster sauce and a small amount of butter.
steps_en.tip: The oyster sauce and butter bring out even more flavor from the thick fried tofu.

steps_en.text: Simmer until the potatoes are tender.
steps_en.tip: Using a drop lid helps the flavors penetrate deeply.

grandma_notes_en: Shirataki doesn't fill you up, so thick fried tofu is ideal for nikujaga because it's hearty and satisfying.
Adding oyster sauce and butter as hidden flavors brings out even more taste from the thick fried tofu.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Taro Stems (Imogara no Nimono)
meta_description_en: Discover Imogara no Nimono, a traditional Japanese preserved food simmered dish from Miyagi. This japanese recipe for dried taro stems reflects the wisdom of home cooking in snow country.
intro_en: Imogara are dried taro stems, a traditional preserved ingredient cherished across the Tohoku region. This gentle simmered dish showcases the resourceful food preservation wisdom of northern Japan, turning humble dried stems into a comforting everyday side dish.
story_en: Imogara are dried taro stems that have been a treasured preserved food in Miyagi and throughout the Tohoku region since ancient times. This was a regular dinner item and also served as a quick side dish that could be offered to guests.
cultural_background_en: Imogara (dried taro stems) are a traditional preserved food that has been used across the Tohoku region for centuries. The practice of drying taro stems under the eaves in autumn to store as winter provisions reflects the ingenuity of people living in snow country.
episode_en: Every autumn, my grandmother would hang the taro stems from her garden to dry in the yard. Seeing them lined up under the eaves was a sign that winter was coming. As a child, I thought it was a plain side dish, but as an adult, I find its gentle flavor strangely comforting. Grandma always said 'this kind of food is good for you,' and she'd make it for me even when I had a cold. I make it myself now, but I can never quite match her flavor.

items.name: Aburaage (thin fried tofu)
items.note: Blanch to remove excess oil, then cut into strips

items.name: Dashi stock (shiitake soaking liquid or bonito dashi)
items.grandma_amount: enough to just cover the ingredients

steps_en.text: Rehydrate the dried imogara in water, wash well, and cut into easy-to-eat lengths. Cut carrot into thin strips, blanch the fried tofu and cut into strips, and rehydrate and slice the dried shiitake.
Heat oil in a pot, lightly stir-fry the ingredients, then add dashi stock and simmer.
Add soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and sake. Simmer gently on low heat until the liquid reduces and the flavors are well absorbed.
steps_en.tip: Slow simmering on low heat lets the flavors penetrate deeply.

grandma_notes_en: Use about a handful of imogara and half a carrot. Add sugar until it's slightly sweet, and soy sauce just enough to color. Add dashi stock to just cover the ingredients.
Grandma always said 'this kind of food is good for you,' and she'd make it even when we had colds.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Takuan Pickles (Takuan-ni)
meta_description_en: Discover Takuan-ni, a traditional Japanese home cooking recipe from Ishikawa. This japanese recipe transforms aged pickled daikon into a gentle simmered dish, embodying zero-waste food wisdom.
intro_en: Takuan-ni transforms over-fermented takuan pickles into a tender, savory simmered dish. This ingenious traditional japanese food from Ishikawa reflects the age-old philosophy of wasting nothing, giving new life to pickles that have become too sour to eat on their own.
story_en: This is a home-style dish from Ishikawa Prefecture where aged takuan pickles are transformed into a simmered side dish. Even when the takuan has become quite sour, simmering it in dashi stock with seasonings gives it a gentle, comforting flavor. It was a regular presence on the dinner table.
cultural_background_en: Simmering aged takuan pickles is a home cooking tradition found in the Hokuriku region, where pickling culture runs deep. The practice of cooking overly fermented pickles rather than discarding them embodies the traditional Japanese spirit of not wasting food.
episode_en: I didn't like the sour taste of aged takuan pickles, but when grandma cooked them this way, I could eat them happily. I remember asking for seconds of rice because of this simple, rustic dish.

items.name: Aged takuan pickles (pickled daikon)
items.note: Slice into thin rounds (about 5mm) and soak in water

items.name: Dashi stock
items.grandma_amount: enough to just cover the takuan

items.name: Dried red chili pepper, sliced into rings

steps_en.text: Slice the aged takuan into thin rounds (about 5mm) and soak in plenty of water. Taste periodically until enough saltiness has been removed.
steps_en.tip: Adjust the salt removal to your preference. Tasting as you go is the key.

steps_en.text: Once enough salt has been removed, place the takuan in a pot with water and parboil.
Add dashi stock, parboiled takuan, soy sauce, sugar, mirin, and sake to the pot and simmer.
Simmer gently on low heat, then add the sliced chili pepper and cook for about 3 more minutes. Serve with grated ginger on the side if desired.
steps_en.tip: Adding the chili near the end prevents the heat from becoming too strong.

grandma_notes_en: Aged takuan is very salty, so soaking it in water and tasting to check the salt level is important.
The chili adds a nice kick as an accent, so add it at the end.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Eggplant Miso Stir-Fry (Nasu Miso Itame)
meta_description_en: A beloved summer japanese recipe from Niigata: eggplant and pork stir-fried in sweet miso sauce. This traditional japanese food is a staple home cooking dish that goes perfectly with steamed rice.
intro_en: This miso stir-fry is a summer staple from Niigata, where eggplant is at its peak from summer through autumn. Grandma's secret? Use plenty of oil so the eggplant becomes silky and tender, then coat everything in a sweet-savory miso glaze.
story_en: Eggplant and pork stir-fried with miso is the perfect summer dish. In Niigata Prefecture, eggplant is in season from summer to autumn, and this was a dinner table regular. Using plenty of oil is the secret to great flavor, and the sweet-salty miso goes perfectly with rice.
cultural_background_en: Miso-stir-fried eggplant is a home-style dish found throughout Japan, but Niigata is particularly known for its excellent eggplant. During the peak summer season, it appears frequently on family dinner tables as a beloved everyday staple.
episode_en: This appeared at dinner every summer. As a child, I sometimes thought 'eggplant again?' but it went so perfectly with rice that it was one of my favorite dishes. I still remember grandma saying 'if you skimp on the oil, the eggplant won't taste good.' The memory of watching her cook in the kitchen and her letting me taste-test the freshly made dish is precious to me.

items.name: Eggplant (Nasu)
items.note: Cut into large pieces and soak in water

items.name: Sugar
items.note: Add a bit more if you prefer it sweeter
items.grandma_amount: a bit more if you like it sweet

items.name: Vegetable oil
items.note: Eggplant absorbs oil, so be generous

steps_en.text: Cut the eggplant into large pieces and briefly soak in water.
Add a generous amount of oil to a frying pan and slowly cook the eggplant until tender. Set aside on a plate.
steps_en.tip: Grandma always said: 'If you skimp on the oil, the eggplant won't taste good.'

steps_en.text: Stir-fry the pork and garlic. Once cooked through, add the green peppers and the reserved eggplant, and heat together.
Add the miso, sugar, mirin, and sake, coating everything evenly. A slight char on the edges is ideal.
steps_en.tip: A slight char adds a wonderful aroma.

grandma_notes_en: Don't skimp on the oil with eggplant. Using a generous amount and cooking slowly is the key.
About a heaping tablespoon of miso. Add a bit more sugar if you like it sweet. One swirl each of mirin and sake.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Butterbur (Fuki no Nimono)
meta_description_en: A springtime japanese recipe for simmered butterbur (fuki) from Saga, Kyushu. This traditional japanese food captures the essence of spring with delicate home cooking techniques passed down through generations.
intro_en: Fuki (Japanese butterbur) is one of the first wild plants to herald spring in Japan. This delicate simmered dish from Saga Prefecture captures the essence of the season with just a few ingredients, letting the fresh, slightly bitter flavor of the butterbur shine.
story_en: Freshly picked butterbur, lightly simmered in dashi and light soy sauce -- this is a spring specialty from a grandmother in Saga Prefecture. She would make it every time her grandchild returned home from the Kansai region, a nostalgic seasonal treat available only during this brief window.
cultural_background_en: Butterbur is a wild plant native to Japan and one of the first signs of spring. Picking fresh butterbur growing near mountainsides and gardens to cook at home is a cherished springtime tradition in rural Japan.
episode_en: I still vividly remember climbing the hills behind grandma's house with her every spring break to gather butterbur. She always said fresh butterbur must be cooked quickly before the bitterness sets in. After not being able to visit during spring for a couple of years, grandma sent me raw butterbur by mail and my mother tried to cook it, but it was too bitter and I couldn't stand it for a while. When I finally visited again in spring and saw butterbur on the table, I was hesitant, but one bite brought back all my happy memories. Now that I live in Saga myself, I make this grandma-taught recipe on my own!

steps_en.text: Rub the fresh butterbur with salt on a cutting board (itazuri), then boil for about 5 minutes. Peel the skin afterward.
steps_en.tip: Board-rubbing helps draw out the bitterness.

steps_en.text: Cut the butterbur into pieces about 4cm long, then simmer in dashi and sake for about 5 minutes.
When the dashi has reduced, add light soy sauce and adjust the flavor while continuing to cook.
steps_en.tip: Using light soy sauce keeps the butterbur's color beautifully green.

grandma_notes_en: Butterbur must be cooked fresh; otherwise the bitterness takes over. Cook it as soon as possible after picking.
Use enough dashi to half-cover the butterbur. About 1 tablespoon each of sake and light soy sauce.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Sweet Soy Sauce Mochi with Nori (Isobe-yaki)
meta_description_en: A sweet-and-salty grilled mochi recipe from Nagano. This traditional japanese food features toasted rice cakes brushed with sugary soy sauce and wrapped in nori seaweed -- a simple home cooking snack.
intro_en: Isobe-yaki is a classic Japanese way to enjoy grilled mochi wrapped in nori seaweed, but this Nagano grandma's version takes it to another level with an extra-sweet soy sauce glaze. The sweet-salty combination hits you with an unforgettable punch of flavor.
story_en: While isobe-yaki typically features a simple soy sauce and nori combination, this version from Suzaka City, Nagano uses a soy sauce glaze loaded with sugar. Served at family gatherings and for lunch, the sweet-salty flavor makes a bold impression.
cultural_background_en: Isobe-yaki gets its name from the 'scent of the shore' (iso) provided by the nori seaweed. The sweet-salty soy sauce style seen in parts of Nagano may be connected to the gohei-mochi tradition of the Chubu region, where sweet-savory glazes are a regional hallmark.
episode_en: Sweet-salty flavors are trendy now, but back then, this was the first time I'd ever eaten such a sweet isobe-yaki. It's a flavor that hits your brain with a wallop.

items.name: Soy sauce
items.grandma_amount: soy sauce to sugar ratio about 5:5 or 4:6

items.name: Sugar
items.note: Lots of sugar is the key

steps_en.text: Toast the mochi in a toaster oven. While it's toasting, mix the soy sauce and sugar together.
steps_en.tip: Use plenty of sugar. The soy sauce to sugar ratio should be about 5:5 or even 4:6.

steps_en.text: Brush the toasted mochi with the sweet soy sauce mixture and wrap with nori seaweed.

grandma_notes_en: She always put loads of sugar in the soy sauce mixture. The ratio felt like about 5:5 or 4:6 soy sauce to sugar.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Nikujaga with Beef (Shimane-Style)
meta_description_en: An authentic Shimane-style Nikujaga japanese recipe using beef and a careful seasoning order. This traditional japanese food showcases the home cooking wisdom of letting flavors rest and deepen overnight.
intro_en: This nikujaga from Shimane Prefecture uses beef, as is typical in western Japan. The secret lies in adding sweet seasonings first and letting the dish rest to deepen its flavor. Grandma never used measuring spoons, yet it always tasted the same.
story_en: Nikujaga is the quintessential Japanese home-cooked dish. In Shimane, beef is commonly used. This recipe's keys are adding sweet seasonings before salty ones and letting the dish cool once before reheating to serve. Despite never using measuring spoons, grandma always produced the same perfect flavor.
cultural_background_en: Nikujaga is one of Japan's most representative home-cooked dishes, said to have been created in the Meiji era. In western Japan, beef is the traditional choice, and Shimane is no exception. The technique of adding seasonings in a specific order and resting the dish to let flavors absorb reflects wisdom passed down through generations.
episode_en: On nikujaga days, the sweet soy sauce aroma would fill the kitchen, and I'd immediately know what was for dinner. Grandma would laugh and say 'the flavor hasn't set yet, be patient' when I tried to sneak a taste. The next day's nikujaga was even more flavorful, and the whole family looked forward to it. It's a simple dish, but whenever the family gathered, it would naturally appear on the table -- the ultimate comfort food.

items.name: Potatoes
items.grandma_amount: a bit more than the number of people

items.name: Onions
items.grandma_amount: enough to spread across the pot

items.name: Water
items.grandma_amount: until the ingredients just peek out

items.name: Oil
items.grandma_amount: a thin layer on the pot bottom

steps_en.text: Peel the potatoes, cut into large pieces, and soak in water. Cut carrots into rolling wedges, onions into wedges. Parboil the shirataki noodles and cut into manageable lengths.
Heat oil in a pot over medium heat and stir-fry the beef first.
steps_en.tip: Grandma said 'just until it changes color is fine.'

steps_en.text: Add onions and carrots, stir-fry lightly until coated in oil, then add potatoes and shirataki.
Add water so the ingredients are barely peeking out. Once boiling, skim off any foam.
Add seasonings in this order: sugar, sake, mirin, then soy sauce.
steps_en.tip: Grandma's teaching: 'Adding sweet things first helps the flavor soak in.'

steps_en.text: Place a drop lid on top, then simmer on medium-low heat for 15-20 minutes. Shake the pot occasionally to prevent sticking.
Once the potatoes are tender, turn off the heat and let it rest to allow flavors to meld. Gently reheat before serving for even better taste.
steps_en.tip: Don't overcook or the potatoes will fall apart. Letting it cool once helps the flavors absorb.

grandma_notes_en: Her catchphrase was 'roughly is fine' -- she hardly ever used measuring spoons, yet it always tasted the same.
Always add sweet seasonings before salty ones.
She always said it tastes better after it sits for a while.
source_types_en: Grandma's handwritten recipe notes still exist

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Sake Lees Soup with Salmon (Kasujiru)
meta_description_en: A warming winter japanese recipe for Kasujiru, sake lees soup from Hyogo. This traditional japanese food from Japan's premier sake-brewing region features salmon and root vegetables in rich home cooking style.
intro_en: Kasujiru is a warming winter soup made with sake lees (kasu), beloved across the Kansai region. Hyogo Prefecture, home to the famous Nada Gogou sake district, has deep ties to this soup. The combination of rich sake lees and salted salmon creates a deeply comforting bowl.
story_en: Kasujiru is a winter soup made by dissolving sake lees into dashi broth, widely enjoyed in the Kansai region and especially in Hyogo Prefecture. The proximity to Nada Gogou, one of Japan's premier sake-brewing districts, meant fresh sake lees were readily available. Adding salted salmon gives the broth extra depth and warms you from the inside out.
cultural_background_en: Kasujiru has been a beloved winter soup particularly in the Kansai region, where sake brewing is deeply rooted. Hyogo's Nada Gogou district is renowned as one of Japan's top sake-producing areas, and using fresh sake lees from the new brewing season to make kasujiru is a winter tradition. Adding salted salmon is a characteristic Kansai touch.
episode_en: In winter, grandma's kitchen was always filled with the sweet aroma of sake lees. Her glasses would fog up from the steam rising from the big pot, and she'd laugh saying 'I can't see!' while taste-testing. When we came home looking cold, we'd hear her kind voice through the steam saying 'eat this and you'll warm right up!' It was packed with ingredients, and by the time we finished, we were warm to our core, feeling as safe as being wrapped in grandma's arms. Even now, when I smell kasujiru, I'm transported back to those winter evenings and grandma's smile.

items.name: Daikon radish
items.grandma_amount: cut thin and plenty of it

items.name: Konnyaku (konjac)
items.grandma_amount: tearing by hand lets the flavor soak in better

items.name: Salted salmon
items.grandma_amount: it gives great flavor, put it in

items.name: Sake lees (Sakekasu)
items.note: 120-150g. Dissolve in a separate bowl with some dashi before adding
items.grandma_amount: taste as you go and add as much as you like

steps_en.text: Cut daikon and carrot into thin quarter-rounds. Tear the konnyaku into pieces by hand.
steps_en.tip: Tearing konnyaku by hand helps it absorb flavor better.

steps_en.text: Add dashi to the pot and simmer the daikon, carrot, and konnyaku over medium heat.
steps_en.tip: Grandma always said: 'Don't rush root vegetables -- cook them slow and gentle.'

steps_en.text: Once the vegetables are slightly tender, add the salmon and continue simmering.
Dissolve the sake lees in a separate bowl with a little dashi, then gradually add it to the pot.
steps_en.tip: Grandma always warned: 'If you dump it all in at once, it'll get lumpy, so be careful.'

steps_en.text: Dissolve the miso into the soup and adjust the seasoning.
Scatter chopped green onions on top to finish.
steps_en.tip: Warming gently on low heat at the end brings the flavors together beautifully.

grandma_notes_en: She'd dissolve the sake lees while tasting, adding as much as she wanted.
'Don't rush root vegetables -- cook them slow and gentle' was her mantra.
Always dissolve the sake lees separately first, then add gradually.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Eggplant with Miso Glaze (Nasu no Abura-Miso)
meta_description_en: A summer favorite japanese recipe from Nagano: eggplant and shishito peppers stir-fried with Shinshu miso. This traditional japanese food is a beloved home cooking side dish that makes rice irresistible.
intro_en: Nagano is famous for its Shinshu miso, and this abura-miso is a summer staple that showcases it beautifully. Garden-fresh eggplant is pan-fried in generous oil until silky, then coated in a sweet miso glaze. Grandma always said this single dish could keep you eating rice bowl after bowl.
story_en: Nagano Prefecture is known as the home of Shinshu miso, and miso-based stir-fries have long been a summer household staple. Freshly picked eggplant is sizzled in oil and coated with miso -- this 'abura-miso' is the ultimate summer side dish. Grandma's trick was to use plenty of oil to make the eggplant meltingly tender.
cultural_background_en: In Nagano, summer brings an abundance of eggplant and shishito peppers from home gardens, and combining them with Shinshu miso for stir-fries has been a family tradition for generations. The sweetness and depth of Shinshu miso pairs perfectly with oil-soaked eggplant.
episode_en: Whenever I visited grandma's house, the kitchen always smelled of slightly charred miso. She'd say 'in summer, this is all you need to eat bowls and bowls of rice' while vigorously stir-frying in her big ladle. I loved how the eggplant soaked up all that oil and became meltingly tender. As a kid, biting into a hot shishito pepper and making a big fuss was a great memory too. Even now, every summer I remember that iron wok's sound and grandma's hunched back, and I try to recreate her dish myself.

items.name: Vegetable oil
items.note: About 3 tablespoons. Being generous with oil is the key

steps_en.text: Remove the stems from the eggplant, cut in half lengthwise, then into diagonal 1cm slices. Do not soak in water.
steps_en.tip: Not soaking in water is grandma's way.

steps_en.text: Poke a hole in each shishito pepper with your finger to prevent them from bursting.
Heat a generous amount of oil in a frying pan over high heat. Stir-fry the eggplant thoroughly until it absorbs the oil and becomes soft.
steps_en.tip: Grandma used a heavy iron wok. Plenty of oil is the secret.

steps_en.text: Add the shishito peppers and toss briefly.
Lower the heat slightly, add the miso and sugar directly to the pan, and use the vegetables' moisture to dissolve and coat everything.
When the miso starts to char slightly and smells fragrant, it's done.

grandma_notes_en: Don't soak the eggplant in water -- that's grandma's way.
Use plenty of oil -- that's the secret to silky eggplant.
Her catchphrase: 'In summer, this is all you need to eat bowls and bowls of rice.'

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Mackerel with Pickled Plum (Saba no Nitsuke)
meta_description_en: A rich simmered mackerel japanese recipe from Hyogo featuring pickled plum and ginger. This traditional japanese food uses raw cane sugar for deep flavor -- an authentic home cooking masterpiece.
intro_en: This mackerel simmered dish from Hyogo stands out for its generous use of pickled plum (umeboshi) and ginger, plus the distinctive depth of raw cane sugar (zaratou). Grandma would patiently baste the fish with simmering liquid until it became glossy and rich.
story_en: While simmered mackerel is enjoyed across Japan, this Hyogo version is distinguished by its generous use of pickled plum and ginger, plus the rich sweetness of raw cane sugar (zaratou). The dish is finished by repeatedly spooning the simmering liquid over the fish until it becomes thick and glossy.
cultural_background_en: Simmered mackerel is found in homes throughout Japan, but adding pickled plum is a traditional technique that both removes fishy odor and adds flavor. Using raw cane sugar (zaratou) is a practice found in some Kansai households, producing a richer sweetness than regular white sugar.
episode_en: Grandma always said 'if you keep touching the fish, it'll fall apart -- be patient and just watch' as she stared intently at the pot. Now that I work in the food wholesale business, I think of her simmered mackerel every time I see good mackerel at the market. It wasn't the refined, light flavor of modern restaurants, but the tang of pickled plum and the depth of zaratou's sweetness paired perfectly with white rice.

items.name: Water
items.grandma_amount: a little less than enough to cover

steps_en.text: Blanch the mackerel: pour hot water over the fillets in a strainer, then gently wash off the white residue and blood under cold water.
Add water, sake, sugar, soy sauce, sliced ginger, and torn umeboshi to a pot and bring to a boil over high heat.
Place the mackerel skin-side up in the boiling liquid. Once it returns to a boil, reduce to medium heat, add a drop lid, and simmer for about 10 minutes.
Remove the drop lid and repeatedly spoon the simmering liquid over the mackerel until it becomes slightly thick and glossy.
steps_en.tip: Keep the heat at medium throughout -- don't lower it. 'If you keep touching the fish, it'll fall apart, so be patient and just watch,' grandma would say.

grandma_notes_en: 'If you keep touching the fish, it'll fall apart, so be patient and just watch' -- she'd say this while staring intently at the pot.
She insisted on using raw cane sugar (zaratou) for its richer sweetness.
Keep medium heat throughout and finish quickly.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Tomato Pork Miso Soup (Tomato Tonjiru)
meta_description_en: A unique Fukushima japanese recipe combining tomatoes with classic pork miso soup. This creative traditional japanese food showcases home cooking innovation where tomato acidity meets rich miso broth.
intro_en: Tonjiru (pork miso soup) is a beloved comfort food across Japan, but this Fukushima grandmother added her own creative twist: lots of fresh tomatoes. The natural acidity of tomatoes combined with miso creates an unexpectedly harmonious flavor, finished with a touch of mirin for depth.
story_en: While pork miso soup is enjoyed throughout Japan, this Fukushima version stands out by adding generous amounts of tomatoes. The miso flavor combined with tomato acidity, finished with mirin for depth, creates grandma's signature bowl.
cultural_background_en: Pork miso soup has roots in hearty miso soups from across Japan, and the pronunciation varies by region. Fukushima is among Japan's top producers of summer-autumn tomatoes, and while adding tomatoes to miso soup has gained recent attention, farming families may have been doing this for much longer. The glutamic acid in tomatoes is said to complement dashi beautifully.
episode_en: Grandma was an incredibly creative cook with many original recipes, all remarkably easy to make. One day when I asked what was for dinner, she simply said 'a refreshing miso soup.' Out came this tomato pork miso soup. I wasn't a big tomato fan at the time, but one taste and I was instantly converted. The harmony of miso and tomato was truly surprising. She later taught me the recipe step by step, saying 'it's really simple, want to try?' -- a wonderful memory.

steps_en.text: Fill a regular pot with water and place on the stove.
While waiting for the water to boil, cut the ingredients. Chop tomatoes into small bite-sized pieces, and roughly cut the negi. Use the green parts too. Set the tomato and negi aside on a tray.
Once the water boils, dissolve the miso first. With the plain miso broth ready, add the tomatoes and negi.
steps_en.tip: Dissolving the miso first is the key step.

steps_en.text: Gently stir with a ladle to blend the miso soup and ingredients together. About 5 minutes.
Once the tomato and negi flavors have infused the broth, add the pork.
Cook the pork through, being careful not to overcook -- about 3 minutes is enough, or the meat will toughen.
steps_en.tip: Overcooking makes the meat tough.

steps_en.text: Once no pink remains on the pork, add the mirin last and gently stir to complete the tomato pork miso soup.
steps_en.tip: The mirin's richness harmonizes wonderfully with the tomato flavor and miso broth.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Sweet Simmered Sweet Potato and Apple (Satsumaimo to Ringo no Amani)
meta_description_en: A cozy autumn japanese recipe from Mie for sweet potato and apple simmered together. This traditional japanese food uses just a touch of soy sauce to add depth to a naturally sweet home cooking treat.
intro_en: This gentle dish from a Mie Prefecture grandmother brings together two autumn favorites -- sweet potato and apple -- simmered until tender with sugar and just a whisper of soy sauce. It works as both a side dish and a snack, capturing the warmth of the harvest season.
story_en: Sweet potato and apple simmered together in a simple, rustic dish. This Mie grandmother often made it from autumn through winter as both a side dish and a snack. A tiny drizzle of soy sauce at the end adds a subtle depth to the sweetness.
cultural_background_en: Sweet potatoes were popularized in Japan during the Edo period by Aoki Konyo as a famine countermeasure. The apple's citric acid enhances the sweet potato's natural sweetness, meaning less sugar is needed -- a classic example of traditional culinary wisdom.
episode_en: Grandma rarely measured anything precisely. She'd say 'this much is about right' while cooking efficiently by feel. With this sweet potato and apple dish especially, even though the sugar seemed like too much, the finished product always had the perfect sweetness. It was one of her little mysteries.

items.name: Sweet potato (Satsumaimo)
items.grandma_amount: cut into big chunks and toss them in

items.name: Water
items.grandma_amount: enough to barely cover the ingredients

steps_en.text: Cut the sweet potato into rough chunks with skin on, and soak in water for about 5 minutes to remove bitterness.
Cut the apple (skin on) into 8 pieces. Remove the core but keep the pieces large.
Place the sweet potato and apple in a pot, adding just enough water to barely cover. Don't add too much.
steps_en.tip: Using less water prevents the pieces from falling apart and concentrates the sweetness.

steps_en.text: Add sugar and place on medium heat. Once boiling, reduce to medium-low, add a drop lid, and simmer gently.
After 10-15 minutes, when the sweet potato is tender, add just a tiny drizzle of soy sauce. It adds a subtle depth to the sweetness.
steps_en.tip: Too much soy sauce changes the character -- 'just a little' is essential.

steps_en.text: Simmer for about 5 more minutes until the flavors meld, then remove from heat. Letting it cool slightly allows the flavors to absorb further.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Kinpira Gobo (Stir-Fried Burdock Root)
meta_description_en: A classic japanese recipe for Kinpira Gobo from Miyagi. This traditional japanese food side dish of stir-fried burdock root and carrot is a timeless home cooking staple found in every Japanese household.
intro_en: Kinpira Gobo is one of Japan's most iconic side dishes -- crisp-tender burdock root and carrot stir-fried in a sweet soy-mirin glaze. This Miyagi grandmother's version uses a generous amount of burdock root and finishes slightly sweet. Burdock root is rarely eaten outside of Japan, making this a truly unique Japanese dish.
story_en: Kinpira gobo is a beloved side dish found across Japan. This Miyagi version is characterized by a generous amount of burdock root that fills the frying pan, with a slightly sweet finish.
cultural_background_en: Kinpira gobo has been a Japanese staple since the Edo period, named after the legendary strongman Sakata Kinpira. Burdock root is extremely rich in dietary fiber and is one of the few countries where it's eaten as food. Each family and region has their own sweet or savory variation, and it's one of the most common make-ahead sides found in Japanese refrigerators.
episode_en: I learned this recipe when I visited grandma's house around middle school. I remember my clumsy attempt at shaving the burdock root. Watching me struggle, grandma kept worrying and repeating 'don't cut your hand.' The finished dish was slightly sweet.

items.name: Gobo (burdock root)
items.grandma_amount: enough to fill the frying pan

items.name: Carrot
items.grandma_amount: about the same amount as the burdock

steps_en.text: Wash the carrot and burdock root well. Cut the carrot into thin strips. Shave the burdock root into thin strips (sasagaki style) and soak in water for about 10 minutes to remove bitterness.
Drain the burdock root in a strainer and pat dry.
Heat 1 tablespoon of sesame oil in a frying pan over medium heat.
Add the burdock root and carrot, stir-frying over medium heat until slightly softened.
Pour 2 tablespoons each of soy sauce and mirin along the edge of the pan and stir-fry over medium heat.
Continue stir-frying until the liquid has evaporated.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Offal Omu-Soba (Horumon Omu-Soba)
meta_description_en: A hearty Wakayama japanese recipe for yakisoba with offal wrapped in egg. This bold traditional japanese food represents resourceful home cooking that filled hungry bellies during summer break.
intro_en: This bold, satisfying dish from Wakayama combines yakisoba noodles with flavorful offal (horumon), wrapped in a thin egg layer and topped with bonito flakes. Grandma invented this budget-friendly lunch to keep her hungry grandson full during summer vacation, using three whole packs of noodles for a single serving.
story_en: A hearty lunch creation from a Wakayama grandmother who needed to fill her big-eating grandson during summer break. Offal gives the noodles rich umami, a blend of yakisoba sauce and Worcestershire sauce adds depth, and the egg wrapping with bonito flakes finishes it off. Three packs of noodles per person was grandma's standard.
cultural_background_en: Horumon (offal) gets its name possibly from 'horu-mon' meaning 'things to throw away,' and became widespread during the post-war food shortage era. The Kansai region, including Wakayama, has a deeply rooted horumon food culture, and adding it to yakisoba or udon on a flat griddle is a quintessential Kansai-style dish.
episode_en: Grandma's house was a small shack, so the kitchen was right next to the table, and I could watch exactly how she cooked. That's how I remember the recipe. She seemed to struggle with what to make for my lunch during summer break. Using three packs of noodles for one person tells you how much I ate as a kid. This was grandma's invention to satisfy my stomach while keeping costs down. Back then her house had no air conditioning, and she'd be sweating while making the yakisoba. Knowing how hard she worked for me, I felt I needed to do my best for her sake. Every summer break lunch was grandma's yakisoba.

items.name: Sauce (blend of yakisoba sauce and Worcestershire sauce)

steps_en.text: Heat oil in a frying pan and stir-fry the horumon.
Once the horumon is partially cooked, add shredded cabbage.
When the cabbage is tender, push the cooked ingredients to one side and add the yakisoba noodles to the empty space.
Carefully push the stir-fried ingredients on top of the noodles and heat for about 2 minutes.
After 2 minutes, flip the whole thing over using the pan.
If the noodles have nice char marks, add the sauce mixture and toss to combine.
Push the noodles to one side, add a little oil to the empty space, and crack an egg onto it.
Lightly break the yolk, then slide the noodles on top of the egg.
Cook for about 45 seconds, then flip the whole thing over.
Plate on a large dish and top with bonito flakes.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Sweet Potato Kakiage Fritters (Gane)
meta_description_en: A Kagoshima japanese recipe for sweet potato kakiage fritters called 'gane.' This traditional japanese food features a uniquely sweet batter -- a home cooking treat that works as both snack and side dish.
intro_en: In Kagoshima, Japan's top sweet potato producing region, these sweet potato fritters are called 'gane' -- named after their crab-leg-like shape. What makes them special is the sugar-loaded batter, creating crispy-outside, chewy-inside fritters that blur the line between snack and side dish.
story_en: In Kagoshima Prefecture, sweet potato kakiage fritters are sometimes called 'gane.' Adding plenty of sugar to the batter is grandma's way, yielding a crispy exterior and chewy interior. These fritters work as both a snack and a side dish.
cultural_background_en: Kagoshima Prefecture is Japan's top sweet potato producer, and the name 'Satsuma' (the old name for Kagoshima) became the common name for the potato itself. The local name 'gane' comes from the fritters' resemblance to crab (gane) legs. Adding sugar to the batter is a distinctly Kagoshima touch, reflecting the region's preference for sweeter flavors.
episode_en: I sometimes still crave that sweet potato flavor. In Kagoshima they call this gane, and it's something like a regional specialty. We'd sneak bites while grandma was still frying, and she'd always scold us. Crispy on the outside, chewy inside -- it's a curious food that's both snack and side dish.

steps_en.text: Cut the sweet potato and carrot into slightly thick matchstick strips.
Place the cut vegetables in a bowl, add flour and sugar, and mix roughly.
Gradually add water until the batter clings thickly to the vegetables.
steps_en.tip: Add water little by little to avoid making it too thin.

steps_en.text: Heat a generous amount of oil in a frying pan. Using chopsticks, form small clusters and drop them into the oil.
Fry on medium heat until both sides are golden brown and crispy.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Round Herring Sushi (Urume-Zushi)
meta_description_en: A festive japanese recipe for salt-and-vinegar-cured round herring sushi from Mie Prefecture. This traditional japanese food was the New Year's centerpiece of a home cooking master's holiday spread.
intro_en: This elegant sushi featuring salt-and-vinegar-cured round herring (urume iwashi) was the crown jewel of a Mie grandmother's New Year's feast. She would prepare dozens of these by hand to feed the entire extended family -- a labor of love from a master of Japanese home cooking and hospitality.
story_en: A Mie Prefecture grandmother who loved cooking and entertaining would prepare this round herring sushi every New Year's to welcome the whole family. The round herring is cured in salt and vinegar until the flesh firms up, then paired with slightly sweet sushi rice. It was the pride of a talented home cook.
cultural_background_en: Round herring (urume iwashi) gets its name from its large, moist-looking eyes ('urumu' meaning to well up). As a fish caught along the Pacific coast including Mie Prefecture, it spoils quickly, so salt-and-vinegar curing is a traditional preservation technique from coastal fishing towns. Serving homemade fish sushi at New Year's is a tradition that reflects gratitude for the ocean's bounty.
episode_en: Grandma was a great cook who loved making sushi and entertaining people. When the whole family gathered for New Year's, she'd be in the kitchen from early morning making enormous amounts of food from mountains of ingredients. Among everything, the round herring sushi was my absolute favorite -- the way the salt and vinegar firmed up the flesh with a slightly strong sour-salty punch paired perfectly with the sweet sushi rice. Even as a child, it was my top sushi.

items.name: Salt
items.note: Enough to coat all the fish

items.name: Salt
items.grandma_amount: she'd put loads in the pot

steps_en.text: Wash the rice and cook in the rice cooker with slightly less water than usual, placing a piece of kombu on top.
steps_en.tip: Slightly less water prevents the sushi rice from becoming mushy.

steps_en.text: Open the round herring by hand, removing the guts and scales. Coat generously with salt and let sit, then drain the liquid. Marinate in a mixture of vinegar and ginger.
Transfer the cooked rice to a wooden sushi tub. Pour over the mixture of vinegar, sugar, and salt, and fold in using cutting motions while fanning to cool.
Once the sushi rice has cooled, form small balls of rice and top each with a drained piece of vinegar-cured herring, wrapping it gently over the rice.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Cold Miso Soup with Extra Tofu (Oi-Dofu Hiyajiru)
meta_description_en: Miyazaki's famous Hiyajiru cold miso soup japanese recipe with a grandma twist: extra tofu added right before serving. This traditional japanese food is the ultimate summer home cooking comfort meal.
intro_en: Hiyajiru is Miyazaki Prefecture's iconic cold miso soup, perfect for beating the summer heat. This grandmother's version features a special 'oi-dofu' (extra tofu) technique -- adding one more block of tofu just before serving for extra heartiness. The base is made by grinding roasted fish and miso in a mortar, torching it for smoky depth, then chilling.
story_en: Hiyajiru is Miyazaki Prefecture's signature summer dish. This version features horse mackerel, barley miso ground in a mortar and charred over direct flame for smokiness. What makes it special is the 'oi-dofu' technique -- adding an extra block of tofu just before eating for volume. Even on days with no appetite, it goes down easy over barley rice.
cultural_background_en: Hiyajiru is a representative regional dish of Miyazaki, with roots possibly tracing back to the Kamakura period. The key step of grinding miso in a mortar then charring it over flame creates the signature smoky flavor. Originally a farmer's quick meal to get nutrition between fieldwork, it's considered an essential part of Miyazaki summers.
episode_en: Whenever I visited during summer, grandma would always bring out this dish along with many others, saying 'eat this, eat lots of this.' A few years ago grandfather passed away, and grandma herself has been eating less due to her age. But I want to eat lots of this hiyajiru in front of her, say 'it's delicious,' and laugh together -- hoping it helps her stay well. I want to gradually return all the love grandma has given us, in whatever ways I can.

items.name: Dried horse mackerel (Aji) or dried barracuda (Kamasu)
Toasted sesame seeds
items.grandma_amount: until you can't see the bottom of the mortar, lots and lots

items.name: Dashi stock (made from dried sardines)
items.grandma_amount: fill a big bowl to the brim, extra because of the ice

items.name: Cucumber
items.grandma_amount: slice thin enough to see through, lots of them

items.name: Firm tofu (Momen-dofu)
items.note: 2 blocks go in first; 1 block added just before serving as 'extra tofu'
items.grandma_amount: crumble by hand, then add one more block at the end

items.name: Shiso (perilla leaves)
items.grandma_amount: loads, until you can smell them

items.name: Myoga (Japanese ginger buds)
items.grandma_amount: loads, until you can smell them

steps_en.text: Grill the dried fish until fragrant, then flake the flesh while still hot, carefully removing all bones.
In a large mortar (suribachi), grind the sesame seeds until the oil comes out, then add the barley miso and continue grinding to combine.
Spread the miso mixture thinly around the inside wall of the mortar and toast it over direct flame until slightly charred and fragrant.
steps_en.tip: Charring over flame dramatically increases the smoky aroma.

steps_en.text: Gradually add chilled dashi stock, dissolving the miso carefully to avoid lumps.
Add the flaked fish, salted and squeezed cucumber slices, and generously crumbled tofu (2 blocks).
Just before serving, crumble one more block of tofu in large pieces as 'oi-dofu' (extra tofu). This adds volume and makes it satisfying even on days with no appetite.
steps_en.tip: The extra tofu is what makes this recipe special.

steps_en.text: Chill well, then top with shredded shiso and sliced myoga just before serving. Pour over barley rice to eat.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Freeze-Dried Tofu (Koya Dofu Fukumeni)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for koya dofu fukumeni - freeze-dried tofu gently simmered in seasoned dashi broth. Authentic home cooking passed down from an Osaka grandmother.
intro_en: Koya dofu (freeze-dried tofu) is a traditional Japanese preserved food with a uniquely spongy texture that soaks up flavorful broth beautifully. This gentle simmered dish from Osaka showcases the deep dashi culture of the Kansai region, combining dried shiitake mushrooms and tofu in a lightly sweet soy-based broth.
story_en: The moment I opened the front door in the evening, that slightly sweet soy sauce aroma would drift toward me, and I'd immediately know: 'It's my favorite koya dofu and shiitake!' Each time, the familiar delicious taste would come flooding back, and I couldn't wait to sit down at the table.
cultural_background_en: Osaka has a deeply rooted dashi (stock) culture, and simmered dishes using dried preserved ingredients are a staple of everyday home cooking. This dish combines two traditional Japanese preserved foods - koya dofu (freeze-dried tofu) and dried shiitake mushrooms - creating a deeply savory and nourishing meal.
episode_en: The moment I opened the front door in the evening, that slightly sweet soy sauce aroma would drift toward me, and I'd immediately know: 'It's my favorite koya dofu and shiitake!' Each time, the familiar delicious taste would come flooding back, and I couldn't wait to sit down at the table.

items.name: Carrot
items.note: Thinly sliced into quarter-rounds (ginkgo leaf cuts)

steps_en.text: Soak the koya dofu briefly in water, squeeze out the excess, and cut into bite-sized pieces. Rehydrate the dried shiitake mushrooms in water.
Bring the dashi stock to a boil in a pot. Add the mirin, sake, sugar, salt, and soy sauce. Add the koya dofu, shiitake mushrooms, and thinly sliced carrot, and bring to a boil.
Once boiling, reduce to low heat and simmer for 20 minutes.
Turn off the heat and leave the pot to cool as-is, allowing the flavors to soak in.
steps_en.tip: The flavors penetrate deeply as the dish cools down - this resting step is key

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Simmered Butterbur with Chicken (Fuki no Nimono)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for fuki no nimono - wild butterbur simmered with chicken in savory dashi broth. Authentic spring home cooking from a Miyagi grandmother.
intro_en: Fuki (Japanese butterbur) is one of the few vegetables native to Japan, and its arrival signals the start of spring. This simmered dish from Miyagi in the Tohoku region pairs the slightly bitter, aromatic butterbur with tender chicken thigh and fried tofu skin in a savory bonito dashi broth.
story_en: At grandma's house, butterbur grew alongside bamboo shoots, myoga ginger, and wild angelica sprouts. Whenever I visited, she would cook them for me. I make this dish myself now, but I can never quite match grandma's flavor.
cultural_background_en: Fuki (butterbur) is one of the few vegetables native to Japan, growing wild from Hokkaido to Kyushu. In the Tohoku region including Miyagi Prefecture, spring brings a burst of wild mountain vegetables - butterbur, bamboo shoots, and angelica sprouts all emerge at once. Dishes made from these foraged ingredients are considered a beloved herald of spring on the Japanese table.
episode_en: At grandma's house, butterbur grew alongside bamboo shoots, myoga ginger, and wild angelica sprouts. Whenever I visited, she would cook them for me. I make this dish myself now, but I can never quite match grandma's flavor.

items.name: Fuki (Japanese butterbur stalks)
items.grandma_amount_en: Whatever was harvested from the garden

items.name: Chicken thigh
items.note: Sear the surface, then cut into bite-sized pieces

items.name: Bonito dashi stock
items.grandma_amount_en: She made her own dashi from bonito flakes - the right amount

steps_en.text: Prepare the butterbur: rub with salt on a cutting board, blanch, cool in cold water, peel, and cut into bite-sized pieces.
steps_en.tip: Rolling the stalks in salt on a cutting board makes peeling much easier

steps_en.text: Cut the fried tofu skin (aburaage) into short strips.
Sear the chicken thigh on the surface, then cut into bite-sized pieces.
steps_en.tip: Searing first seals in the umami flavor

steps_en.text: Combine the bonito dashi, sake, mirin, and soy sauce in a pot and bring to a boil. Add the chicken and fried tofu skin.
Once the chicken is cooked through, add the butterbur and simmer on low heat for 20-30 minutes.
Let it cool and rest to allow the flavors to soak in.
steps_en.tip: Leaving it overnight lets the flavors meld even more deeply

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Miso Oden with Daikon and Konnyaku (Miso Oden)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for Aichi-style miso oden - daikon radish and konnyaku simmered in rich hatcho miso. Authentic home cooking from an Aichi grandmother.
intro_en: Aichi Prefecture is famous for its distinctive dark red hatcho miso, and this miso oden is the ultimate winter comfort food from the region. Unlike the light dashi-based oden found elsewhere in Japan, Aichi-style oden features ingredients simmered for days in a sweet, rich miso sauce until they turn nearly black with absorbed flavor.
story_en: This is the jet-black oden unique to Aichi. It's loaded with sugar, so it's much sweeter than it looks. The best part was when the daikon was simmered for several days until it turned dark brown all the way through. My guilty pleasure was pouring the leftover miso sauce over rice at the end - a bit messy, but so good.
cultural_background_en: Hatcho miso originates from Okazaki City in Aichi Prefecture. Made only from soybeans and salt, it is aged for over two years, resulting in a deep color and rich umami. Aichi-style miso oden features this miso as the base, combined with generous amounts of sugar for a sweet-savory sauce. The dish is simmered for days, with each reheating deepening the flavor - a beloved winter tradition in Aichi households.
episode_en: This is the jet-black oden unique to Aichi. It's loaded with sugar, so it's much sweeter than it looks. The best part was when the daikon was simmered for several days until it turned dark brown all the way through. My guilty pleasure was pouring the leftover miso sauce over rice at the end - a bit messy, but so good.

items.name: Daikon radish
items.note: Cut into thick rounds, score crosshatch marks on one side, and parboil

items.name: Konnyaku (konjac)
items.note: Cut into triangles and parboil to remove bitterness

items.name: Aka miso / Hatcho miso (dark red soybean miso)
items.note: Dissolve in a bowl with some cooking liquid before adding to the pot

items.name: Sugar
items.grandma_amount_en: A whole heap - about the same amount as the miso

items.name: Mirin (sweet rice wine)
