items.grandma_amount_en: Just a little splash for shine

steps_en.text: Cut the daikon into thick rounds. Score crosshatch marks on one side, then parboil until slightly tender.
steps_en.tip: The crosshatch scoring helps the miso flavor penetrate deeply

steps_en.text: Cut the konnyaku into triangles and parboil to remove any bitterness.
Place the dashi stock, daikon, konnyaku, and pork in a pot and simmer until everything is tender.
In a separate bowl, dissolve the red miso, sugar, and mirin with some of the cooking liquid, then add this mixture to the pot.
steps_en.tip: Dissolving the miso separately first prevents lumps

steps_en.text: Simmer on low heat until the broth becomes thick and the ingredients have turned very dark from the miso.
steps_en.tip: The longer you simmer (even over several days), the more flavor soaks in

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Daikon Radish with Egg (Daikon no Tamago Toji)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for daikon no tamago toji - tender simmered daikon radish finished with soft egg. Simple home cooking from a Kumamoto grandmother.
intro_en: This humble yet deeply comforting dish from Kumamoto in southern Japan features thin-cut daikon radish simmered in a sweet soy sauce broth, then finished with a soft blanket of beaten egg. It's the kind of simple, nourishing home cooking that Japanese grandmothers have been making for generations.
story_en: This was my favorite of grandma's dishes when I was a picky eater as a child. Knowing how much I loved it, grandma would always say, 'Here, I made your simmered daikon!' I especially loved the parts where the egg clung to the sweet soy sauce. She'd scold me gently: 'You only eat the egg part! Eat the daikon too!' Sometimes she'd make it specially for me because I didn't like fish, and she'd set some aside and say, 'This one's for you' with a big smile. That memory is precious to me.
cultural_background_en: Daikon radish has been an essential vegetable on the Japanese table since ancient times, with the saying 'daikon has twelve faces' reflecting its incredible versatility - it can be simmered, grilled, pickled, and more. In Kyushu, including Kumamoto, many households use a sweeter style of soy sauce. When finished with egg, the broth's umami and the egg's richness create a simple yet deeply satisfying dish.
episode_en: This was my favorite of grandma's dishes when I was a picky eater as a child. Knowing how much I loved it, grandma would always say, 'Here, I made your simmered daikon!' I especially loved the parts where the egg clung to the sweet soy sauce. Sometimes she'd make it specially just for me, and she'd set some aside with a smile - that memory is precious to me.

items.name: Soy sauce
items.grandma_amount_en: Just the right amount by feel

items.name: Mirin (sweet rice wine)
items.grandma_amount_en: Just the right amount by feel

items.name: Sugar
items.grandma_amount_en: Just the right amount by feel

steps_en.text: Cut the daikon into thin strips and place in a pot with enough water to cover.
Add the sugar, soy sauce, and mirin, and simmer over medium heat for 5 minutes.
When it starts bubbling, reduce to low heat and simmer gently for about 20 minutes, until the daikon is very tender.
Pour in the beaten egg, cover with a lid, and cook until the egg is just set.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Carrot Salad with Raisins and Mandarin Oranges (Ninjin Salad)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for carrot salad with raisins and mandarin oranges. A refreshing home cooking side dish from a Tokyo grandmother, perfect for kids.
intro_en: This bright, fruity carrot salad is a creative Japanese grandmother's answer to getting kids to eat their vegetables. By combining finely shredded carrots with sweet raisins and canned mandarin oranges in a citrusy sushi vinegar dressing, the natural bitterness of raw carrots is beautifully tamed.
story_en: It was a home where relatives gathered often. Some of the children didn't like carrots, and I was told grandma wanted to find a way to make carrots delicious for them. I absolutely loved this salad - my mother makes it too, and now that I cook myself, it's become one of my go-to dishes. Whenever I asked grandma about measurements, she'd always say, 'The best amount is whatever feels right!' But between watching her cook and learning amounts from my mother, I've been able to recreate it.
cultural_background_en: Carrots are a leading source of beta-carotene among vegetables, but their distinctive grassy taste makes them one of the most commonly disliked vegetables among children in Japan. Combining them with sweet fruits like mandarin oranges and raisins helps mask the bitterness - a technique used worldwide, from Japanese home cooking to French carrot rapee.
episode_en: It was a home where relatives gathered often. Some of the children didn't like carrots, and grandma wanted to help them enjoy it. I loved this salad, and whenever I asked about measurements, she'd always say, 'The best amount is whatever feels right!' But between watching her cook and learning from my mother, I've recreated it.

items.name: Carrots (large)
items.note: Peeled and cut into very fine julienne strips

items.name: Raisins
items.note: Plumped in hot water, then drained

items.name: Salt
items.note: Toss with the carrots and let sit for 30 minutes

steps_en.text: Peel the carrots and cut into very fine julienne strips. Toss with salt and let sit for about 30 minutes, then drain the released liquid.
steps_en.tip: Don't rinse off the salt - just drain the water that comes out

steps_en.text: Soak the raisins in hot water until plump, then drain.
Drain the canned mandarin oranges, reserving some syrup.
Combine the carrots, raisins, and mandarins. Add the lemon juice, mandarin syrup, and sushi vinegar. Mix well and refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving.
steps_en.tip: Leaving it overnight lets the flavors meld and makes it even more enjoyable

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Scattered Sushi (Chirashi Zushi)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for chirashi zushi - colorful scattered sushi with vegetables and egg. Authentic home cooking for celebrations from a Tokyo grandmother.
intro_en: Chirashi zushi is a festive Japanese dish of vinegared rice topped or mixed with colorful ingredients. Traditionally served during Hinamatsuri (Girls' Day) in March and other celebrations, each family has its own version. This Tokyo grandmother's recipe features vegetables like lotus root, carrot, and shiitake mixed into the sushi rice.
story_en: I remember visiting grandma's house as a child and she made chirashi zushi for me - it was so delicious. We made it together several times, and through those cooking sessions she taught me the joy of cooking. Even now, every March I get an irresistible craving for chirashi zushi.
cultural_background_en: Chirashi zushi is a traditional Japanese dish of vinegared rice with scattered ingredients on top. It is often prepared for celebrations and especially for Hinamatsuri (Girls' Day, March 3). Regional variations are significant - in Tokyo, raw sashimi toppings are common, while in western Japan, ingredients are mixed directly into the rice. Auspicious ingredients like lotus root (symbolizing a clear future) and egg (representing wealth) reflect parents' wishes for their children's health and happiness.
episode_en: I remember visiting grandma's house as a child and she made chirashi zushi for me - it was so delicious. We made it together several times, and she taught me the joy of cooking. Even now, every March I get an irresistible craving for chirashi zushi.

steps_en.text: Finely chop the lotus root, carrot, shiitake mushrooms, and burdock root.

Combine the vinegar, sugar, and salt in a microwave-safe dish and heat for about 15 seconds (or warm in a small pot until dissolved). Mix into the cooked rice. Add the chopped omelet, lotus root, carrot, shiitake, and burdock root, and fold together.
Top with sesame seeds, shredded nori, and any other toppings you like. Serve!

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Flour Dumpling Soup (Suiton Jiru)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for suiton jiru - chewy flour dumplings in savory vegetable soup. Comforting home cooking from a Tokyo grandmother, perfect for cold nights.
intro_en: Suiton is one of Japan's oldest comfort foods - simple flour dumplings torn by hand and simmered in a savory broth with vegetables. Dating back centuries and widely eaten as a filling staple during wartime, this humble dish has been passed down through generations of Japanese grandmothers as the ultimate warming meal.
story_en: I work at an IT company now, sitting at a computer all day, and when I get home late I often have no energy to make a proper meal. That's when I remember this suiton and make it often. I can still picture grandma kneading the dough with her flour-covered hands at home. Even though there's nothing fancy in it, eating those chewy dumplings soaked in broth just makes me feel so at ease.
cultural_background_en: Suiton has been eaten in Japan since the Muromachi period (14th-16th century) and was widely consumed as a rice substitute during wartime. Despite its simple preparation - just flour and water dough dropped into broth - it goes by many regional names: 'hittsumi' in Iwate, 'hatto' in Miyagi, and 'dago-jiru' in Kyushu, each reflecting unique local food traditions.
episode_en: I work in IT now, and when I come home late with no energy to cook, I remember this suiton. I can still picture grandma kneading the dough with her flour-covered hands. Even though there's nothing fancy in it, those chewy dumplings soaked in broth just make me feel so at ease.

items.name: Water (for dough)
items.grandma_amount_en: Until it feels like an earlobe - soft but holds its shape

steps_en.text: Put the flour in a bowl and gradually add water while kneading by hand. When the dough is about as soft as an earlobe, set it aside to rest.
Boil water in a pot. Add the dashi powder, chopped vegetables, and pork, and simmer.
When the vegetables are tender, season with soy sauce.
Tear the rested dough into bite-sized pieces by hand and drop them into the simmering soup.
When the dumplings float to the surface and have simmered for about 5 minutes, they're done!

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Stir-Fried Potato Kinpira (Jagaimo Kinpira)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for jagaimo kinpira - crispy stir-fried potatoes in sweet soy glaze with sesame oil. Quick home cooking from a Hiroshima grandmother.
intro_en: Kinpira is a beloved Japanese cooking technique of stir-frying vegetables in sesame oil with a sweet soy sauce glaze. While burdock root is the classic choice, this Hiroshima grandmother's version uses julienned potatoes for a crispy-on-the-outside, tender-inside texture. The aroma of sesame oil is the signature of this dish.
story_en: Both my parents worked, so grandma often made dinner. As I walked home from school, the closer I got to the house, the stronger the sesame oil aroma would become. Sesame oil has such a distinctive smell - I could always tell it was potato kinpira day, and I'd get excited walking home. Even now, whenever I smell sesame oil, it brings back memories of grandma and my childhood.
cultural_background_en: The name 'kinpira' is said to derive from Sakata Kimpira, a legendary strongman character from Edo-period puppet theater. While burdock root is the classic choice, potato kinpira offers a crispy texture and cooks quickly, making it a popular weeknight side dish. Stir-frying in sesame oil adds a nutty fragrance, and since the dish tastes great even at room temperature, it's perfect for bento lunch boxes.
episode_en: Both my parents worked, so grandma often made dinner. Walking home from school, I'd smell the sesame oil from blocks away and know it was potato kinpira day. Even now, whenever I smell sesame oil, it brings back memories of grandma and my childhood.

items.name: Mentsuyu (concentrated noodle soup base)
items.note: Can substitute with soy sauce + dashi

items.name: Shiro-dashi (light-colored dashi concentrate)
items.note: Can substitute with dashi stock + a little soy sauce

steps_en.text: Cut the potatoes into thin matchsticks and stir-fry in sesame oil.
steps_en.tip: Cook over medium heat for about 7 minutes

steps_en.text: When the potatoes turn golden, add the mirin, mentsuyu, shiro-dashi, and sugar. Stir-fry over medium heat for about 3 more minutes.
When the liquid has fully evaporated, reduce to low heat and sprinkle with white sesame seeds.
steps_en.tip: Mix the sesame seeds in thoroughly

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Chicken Rice (Kashiwa Meshi)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for kashiwa meshi - Fukuoka-style seasoned chicken rice cooked in a rice cooker. Authentic regional home cooking from a Fukuoka grandmother.
intro_en: Kashiwa meshi is a beloved regional rice dish from Fukuoka in southern Japan. In Kyushu, chicken is affectionately called 'kashiwa,' and this flavorful rice features chicken, burdock root, and shiitake mushrooms pre-cooked in a sweet soy glaze before being combined with rice in the cooker.
story_en: In Fukuoka, we call chicken 'kashiwa,' and this seasoned rice was a real staple growing up. The key is cooking the toppings in a sweet-savory glaze first, then adding them to the rice cooker - it means the flavor soaks through every grain. It tastes just as good cold, and grandma would make onigiri rice balls for me to take along. That's one of my favorite memories.
cultural_background_en: Kashiwa meshi is one of the signature dishes of Fukuoka Prefecture. In Kyushu, the word 'kashiwa' refers to chicken, and this seasoned rice is also beloved as an ekiben (train station bento). The technique of pre-cooking the ingredients in a sweet soy glaze before adding them to the rice cooker ensures deeply seasoned, flavorful rice.
episode_en: In Fukuoka, we call chicken 'kashiwa,' and this seasoned rice was a real staple. The key is cooking the toppings first, so the flavor soaks through every grain. It tastes great cold too - grandma would make onigiri for me to take along. That's one of my favorite memories.

items.name: Chicken thigh
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Make sure to put in plenty of kashiwa'

items.name: Gobo (burdock root)
items.note: Shaved into thin strips (sasagaki) and soaked in water

items.name: Dried shiitake mushrooms
items.note: Rehydrated and minced (save the soaking liquid)

steps_en.text: Rehydrate the dried shiitake mushrooms in water and mince. Save the soaking liquid for later.
Cut the chicken thigh into small pieces. Shave the burdock root into thin strips and soak in water to remove bitterness.
Heat a little oil in a frying pan and stir-fry the chicken, burdock root, and shiitake mushrooms.
steps_en.tip: Cook over medium heat until everything is well done

steps_en.text: When cooked through, add the soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and sake. Cook until the liquid has almost completely evaporated and the ingredients are well glazed.
steps_en.tip: Watch carefully to avoid burning - the goal is a rich glaze coating everything

steps_en.text: Put the washed rice in the rice cooker. Add the shiitake soaking liquid, then add water up to the 3-cup mark.
steps_en.tip: Using the mushroom soaking liquid adds a wonderful umami depth

steps_en.text: Place the glazed ingredients (with their remaining sauce) on top of the rice without mixing. Cook on the normal rice setting. Once done, mix everything together thoroughly.
steps_en.tip: Don't mix before cooking - place the ingredients on top and let the rice cooker do the work

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Pumpkin Miso Noodle Stew (Houtou)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for houtou - Yamanashi's famous flat noodle stew with pumpkin and vegetables in miso broth. Hearty home cooking from a Yamanashi grandmother.
intro_en: Houtou is the signature regional dish of Yamanashi Prefecture, featuring thick, flat udon-like noodles simmered directly in a rich miso broth loaded with vegetables. Kabocha pumpkin is the essential ingredient - as it breaks down, it naturally thickens the broth and adds a gentle sweetness. This is the ultimate cold-weather comfort food.
story_en: Grandma would stir the big pot round and round, laughing and saying, 'It's not houtou without pumpkin!' And somehow, on houtou days she always happened to be wearing a white shirt. She'd make it on chilly days or when she wanted us to eat lots of vegetables - it was a guaranteed winter classic.
cultural_background_en: Houtou is the signature dish of Yamanashi Prefecture. It features wide, flat noodles simmered with generous vegetables in miso broth. Kabocha pumpkin is considered essential - as it cooks, it partially dissolves to create the characteristic thick, sweet broth that defines authentic houtou.
episode_en: Grandma would stir the big pot round and round, laughing and saying, 'It's not houtou without pumpkin!' Somehow, she always happened to be wearing a white shirt on houtou days.

items.name: Houtou noodles (wide flat wheat noodles)
items.note: Substitute: wide udon noodles or pappardelle pasta

items.name: Dashi stock
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Same amount as you'd make miso soup with'

steps_en.text: Cut all vegetables into large, easy-to-eat pieces. Hard vegetables like potatoes, pumpkin, daikon, and carrot should be cut on the larger side.
Bring the dashi stock to a boil in a large pot. Add the hard vegetables (potatoes, pumpkin, daikon, carrot, burdock root) first and simmer until very tender.
steps_en.tip: Keep at medium heat for a slow, thorough simmer

steps_en.text: When the vegetables are soft, add the houtou noodles, napa cabbage, green onion, and fried tofu skin.
Add the pork and cook over medium heat until done.
steps_en.tip: Add the meat last so it doesn't get tough

steps_en.text: When everything is cooked through, dissolve the miso into the broth and bring to a brief boil. Serve!
steps_en.tip: The pumpkin breaking down and thickening the broth is what gives houtou its signature character

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Seven Herb Rice Porridge (Nanakusa Gayu)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for nanakusa gayu - the seven herbs rice porridge eaten on January 7th. Authentic home cooking ritual from a Chiba grandmother.
intro_en: Nanakusa gayu is a cherished Japanese New Year tradition - a gentle rice porridge made with seven spring herbs, eaten on January 7th to rest the stomach after rich holiday feasts and to wish for good health in the year ahead. This is one of Japan's most beautiful seasonal food rituals, connecting the table to nature's calendar.
story_en: On January mornings, it was still dark and the kitchen was bitterly cold, but every year I'd be woken by the rhythmic sound of grandma chopping on the cutting board. She'd hum a song while chopping and say with a laugh, 'This will keep us healthy all year!' The porridge that arrived at the table with steam rising from the bowl had a slight bitterness that was challenging for me as a child, but it tasted incredibly gentle and reassuring. I especially loved it when she included tiny vegetables from the garden.
cultural_background_en: Nanakusa gayu is a traditional Japanese dish eaten on January 7th (Jinjitsu no Sekku). It is meant to rest the stomach after rich New Year's celebrations and to wish for good health throughout the coming year. The seven spring herbs - seri (water dropwort), nazuna (shepherd's purse), gogyo (cudweed), hakobera (chickweed), hotokenoza (nipplewort), suzuna (turnip), and suzushiro (daikon radish) - are chopped and stirred into the porridge.
episode_en: On January mornings it was still dark and the kitchen was bitterly cold, but every year I'd be woken by the rhythmic sound of grandma chopping on the cutting board. She'd hum a song and say with a laugh, 'This will keep us healthy all year!' The porridge had a slight bitterness that was challenging as a child, but it tasted incredibly gentle and reassuring.

items.name: Rice
items.note: Wash and drain in a sieve for 30 minutes

items.name: Water
items.note: Adjust depending on how thick you like your porridge
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Fill the pot to just above the halfway mark'

items.name: Nanakusa - Seven spring herbs
items.note: Seri (water dropwort), Nazuna (shepherd's purse), Gogyo (cudweed), Hakobera (chickweed), Hotokenoza (nipplewort), Suzuna (turnip), Suzushiro (daikon)
items.grandma_amount_en: 'A handful of each. One small turnip and one small daikon'

items.name: Salt
items.grandma_amount_en: 'A three-finger pinch, taste and repeat twice'

steps_en.text: Wash the rice and leave it in a sieve to drain for 30 minutes.
steps_en.tip: Grandma said, 'Letting the rice absorb water properly is the secret to fluffy porridge'

steps_en.text: Briefly blanch the seven herbs, cool in cold water, and chop finely.
steps_en.tip: Grandma would chop rhythmically on the cutting board, humming a little tune as she worked

steps_en.text: Put the rice and water in a pot. Start on high heat, then reduce to low once boiling. Leave the lid slightly ajar and cook gently for 40-50 minutes.
steps_en.tip: Don't stir too much - over-stirring makes it gummy

steps_en.text: When the porridge is done, add the chopped herbs and salt. Turn off the heat while the herbs are still bright green - don't overcook them.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Amber Egg Jelly (Bekko)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for bekko - Toyama's amber-colored egg and agar jelly with ginger. A beautiful regional home cooking dish for celebrations.
intro_en: Bekko is a stunning regional specialty from Toyama Prefecture - a shimmering amber-colored jelly made from agar, eggs, soy sauce, and ginger. Named for its resemblance to tortoiseshell (bekko in Japanese), this elegant yet simple dish has been a fixture of celebration tables in Toyama for generations.
story_en: This was always made for New Year's, festivals, and family gatherings. I also have a strong memory of grandma making this when I was sick and had no appetite, saying, 'It slides down smooth and easy.' The way she poured in the beaten egg was so graceful - watching the yellow swirls bloom in the pot like flowers was a sight I always loved to watch from the side.
cultural_background_en: Bekko is a regional specialty of Toyama Prefecture - agar jelly with egg and soy sauce that sets into a beautiful amber color resembling bekko (tortoiseshell). It has been a staple of celebration tables during New Year's and festivals, cherished as a visually elegant dish that brings a festive atmosphere to the table.
episode_en: This was a dish made for celebrations, but grandma also made it when I was sick, saying it was easy to eat. The way she poured in the beaten egg was so graceful - watching the yellow swirls bloom in the pot like flowers was something I always loved to watch. When this amber-colored beauty appeared on the table, I'd feel excited knowing it was a special day.

items.name: Kanten bar (agar stick)
items.note: Soak in water until soft; can substitute with 4g agar powder

items.name: Eggs
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Beat them well, then pour them in so they bloom like flowers'

items.name: Water
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Fill the pot generously, to about the two-knuckle mark'

items.name: Sugar
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Be generous - it tastes best when it's sweet'

items.name: Soy sauce
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Add a little at a time until it's a beautiful amber color'

steps_en.text: Soak the agar stick in water until soft, then tear into small pieces and squeeze out excess water.
Dissolve the agar in water over medium heat. Cook until the agar is completely dissolved with no visible pieces remaining.
Add the sugar and soy sauce. When the liquid turns a beautiful amber color, stir in the grated ginger.
Turn up the heat to high. While the liquid is at a rolling boil, slowly pour in the beaten egg in a thin stream while stirring in circles. The egg will spread out in beautiful wisps.
steps_en.tip: Grandma would pour the egg so delicately that it looked like yellow flowers blooming in the pot

steps_en.text: Pour into a flat container or baking tray and leave in a cool place until fully set. Cut into squares to serve.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Hearty Flour Dumpling Soup (Suiton Jiru - Tochigi Style)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for suiton jiru - filling flour dumpling soup with root vegetables. Hearty home cooking from a Tochigi grandmother, perfect for cold days.
intro_en: This Tochigi version of suiton showcases the dish's roots as a practical, filling meal born from Japanese grandmothers' resourcefulness. When rice was scarce or vegetables needed using up, grandma would whip up a big pot of this hearty soup with whatever was in the fridge, stretching simple ingredients into a complete, satisfying meal.
story_en: This was a dish grandma made year-round whenever rice was running low or there were leftover vegetables. She always made it by eye without measuring anything, which really impressed me. It was packed with vegetables, and she'd say, 'This'll fill you right up!' It was one of the dishes she made most often, and it's a treasured memory.
cultural_background_en: Suiton is a humble dish of flour dumplings in soup, widely known as a wartime rice substitute. But in many households, it has been passed down as beloved everyday home cooking. Making it 'by eye' without measuring is part of the tradition - each grandmother's intuition creating a unique family flavor that cannot be replicated from a recipe alone.
episode_en: Grandma made this whenever rice was running low. She always cooked by eye, which really impressed me. It was packed with vegetables - she'd say, 'This'll fill you right up!' It's one of my most treasured food memories.

items.name: Satoimo (taro root)
items.note: Can substitute with small waxy potatoes

steps_en.text: Slice the vegetables into thin quarter-rounds. Shave the burdock root into thin strips and soak in water to remove bitterness.
In a bowl, combine the flour and salt. Gradually add water while mixing until the batter is smooth.
Put the dashi stock and root vegetables in a pot and bring to a boil. Skim any foam and simmer until vegetables are tender.
Season with soy sauce. You can also add a little sugar or mirin if you like.
Using a wet spoon, scoop bite-sized portions of batter and drop them into the simmering soup. When the dumplings float to the surface, they're done!

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Eggplant and Ground Meat Rice Bowl (Nasu Miso Don)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for nasu miso don - stir-fried eggplant and ground meat rice bowl with ponzu. Quick everyday home cooking from a Saitama grandmother.
intro_en: This quick, satisfying rice bowl is the kind of effortless weekday meal that Japanese grandmothers excel at. Summer eggplant is stir-fried with ground meat and seasoned simply with ponzu (citrus soy sauce), then piled onto hot rice. It takes just 15 minutes from start to finish.
story_en: This was a dish grandma made when I came home from playing outside in the summer. While cooking, she'd also have cucumber salad and homemade snacks ready for me to munch on. Even if my table manners were a little messy, she'd just say, 'It's fine, it's fine' with a gentle smile. That warmth is something I remember fondly.
cultural_background_en: Eggplant is a quintessential summer vegetable in Japan, and its affinity with oil is well known - stir-frying gives it a wonderfully silky texture. Finishing with ponzu (citrus soy sauce) keeps it light and refreshing, making this a perfect midday meal on hot summer days.
episode_en: Grandma would also have cucumber salad and homemade snacks ready while she cooked. Even if my table manners were a little messy, she'd just say, 'It's fine, it's fine' with a gentle smile.

items.name: Japanese eggplant (nasu)
items.note: Cut into bite-sized pieces; can use any eggplant variety

items.name: Ponzu (citrus soy sauce)
items.note: Can substitute with soy sauce + lemon juice

steps_en.text: Cut the eggplant into bite-sized pieces.
Heat oil in a frying pan and stir-fry the eggplant.
Add the ground meat and stir-fry together with the eggplant until cooked through.
Season with salt, pepper, and ponzu. Stir to combine.
Spoon the mixture over the bowl of rice and serve!

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Layered Bonito Flake Rice (Okaka Gohan)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for okaka gohan - layered bonito flake and soy sauce rice bento. Ingenious home cooking from a Wakayama grandmother, perfect for packed lunches.
intro_en: Okaka gohan is the ultimate Japanese comfort food in its simplest form - just rice, bonito flakes, and soy sauce. But grandma's special technique of layering them like a mille-feuille in a bento box transforms these three humble ingredients into something magical. As the bento sits, the umami-rich bonito and soy sauce slowly permeate every grain of rice.
story_en: This was grandpa's lunch bento for summer fishing trips - okaka rice with pickles. After I tried a bite and fell in love with it, grandma started packing okaka rice for my fishing lunches too from then on.
cultural_background_en: Okaka is another name for katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes), one of the foundational ingredients in Japanese cuisine. The technique of layering soy sauce-seasoned bonito flakes between layers of hot rice in a bento box is a piece of Japanese wisdom - over time, the dashi flavor from the bonito gradually soaks into the rice, making it delicious even when cold.
episode_en: This was grandpa's fishing lunch - okaka rice with pickles. After I tried a bite and fell in love, grandma started making it for my lunches too.

items.name: Freshly cooked rice
items.unit: g (7 oz / about 1 generous cup cooked)

steps_en.text: In a small bowl, mix the bonito flakes with soy sauce until well combined.
Place a layer of hot freshly cooked rice in a bento box.
Spread a thin, even layer of the soy sauce bonito flakes over the rice.
Add another layer of hot rice on top, then another layer of bonito flakes. Repeat, building up like a mille-feuille.
steps_en.tip: Layering like a mille-feuille is grandma's special technique

steps_en.text: Let it cool, then close the lid. By lunchtime, the dashi and soy sauce will have soaked into every grain, creating a deeply savory treat.
steps_en.tip: The waiting time is what makes this special - the flavors meld and deepen as it sits

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Niigata Root Vegetable Stew (Noppe)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for noppe - Niigata's signature taro and chicken stew for New Year. Authentic regional home cooking from a Niigata grandmother.
intro_en: Noppe is the quintessential comfort dish of Niigata Prefecture - a hearty stew built around taro root and chicken, traditionally made in a big pot for New Year's, Obon, and family gatherings. Every household in Niigata has its own version, and 'our family's noppe' is a source of quiet pride.
story_en: This was a staple from New Year's Eve through the holiday period, always served alongside the osechi (New Year dishes) at every single meal. Even after days of eating it, I never got tired of the taste. Now as an adult, I find it pairs perfectly with beer. It's a dish the whole family can enjoy together.
cultural_background_en: Noppe is one of the most representative dishes of Niigata Prefecture - a taro-based stew served at New Year's, Obon, and gatherings. It is always made in large quantities, and every household has its own variation in ingredients and seasoning. The concept of 'our family's noppe' is a cherished tradition in Niigata homes.
episode_en: This was a staple through the entire New Year period. Even after days of eating it, I never got tired of the taste. As an adult, it pairs perfectly with beer. A dish the whole family enjoys together.

items.name: Satoimo (taro root)
items.note: Can substitute with small waxy potatoes

steps_en.text: Cut the taro, carrot, and chicken into bite-sized pieces.
Put water and all the ingredients in a pot and bring to a boil.
When the carrot is getting tender, add the seasonings.
steps_en.tip: The chicken releases its own umami-rich broth, so even a rough approach turns out well

steps_en.text: Taste and adjust the seasonings as needed. When the flavors have melded throughout, it's done. Garnish with green beans before serving.

title_en: Japanese Grandma's Bitter Melon Stir-Fry (Goya Champuru)
meta_description_en: A traditional Japanese recipe for goya champuru - Okinawa-style bitter melon stir-fry with pork and egg. Energizing home cooking from a Tokyo grandmother for summer vitality.
intro_en: Goya champuru is Okinawa's most famous home-cooked dish, and it has become a beloved summer staple across all of Japan. 'Champuru' means 'mix it all up' in Okinawan, and this colorful stir-fry of bitter melon, pork, fishcake, and egg is believed to help fight summer fatigue - the bitterness is the key to its energizing power.
story_en: Every year from June to July, grandma would make this for lunch or dinner during the summer months. When I'd visit her house during summer vacation, she'd often cook it for me. I was on the school tennis team at the time, and she'd laugh and say, 'This will prevent heatstroke!' as she made it. Her favorite saying was, 'The more bitter the food, the more energy it gives you.'
cultural_background_en: Goya champuru is the signature dish of Okinawa. 'Champuru' means 'mixed up' in the Okinawan language, and the dish combines tofu, vegetables, and meat in a stir-fry. Goya (bitter melon) is rich in vitamin C and is widely regarded as a natural remedy for summer fatigue, which has helped the dish spread from Okinawa to become popular throughout Japan.
episode_en: Grandma passed away two years ago at 89, but every year as the weather warms in early June, I remember her words - 'The more bitter the food, the more energy it gives you' - and make this dish myself. It's helped me avoid heatstroke and stay healthy through the summer.

items.name: Goya (bitter melon)
items.note: Can be found at Asian grocery stores

items.name: Pork (thinly sliced)
items.grandma_amount_en: 'Just a little bit of pork is enough'

items.name: Chikuwa (tube-shaped fishcake)
items.note: Can substitute with firm tofu or other fishcake

steps_en.text: Cut the bitter melon in half lengthwise, scoop out the seeds and white pith with a spoon, and slice thinly. Cut the chikuwa on the diagonal.
Heat oil in a frying pan and cook the pork first, taking time to cook it through thoroughly.
steps_en.tip: Grandma's rule was to always cook the pork first and take her time with it

steps_en.text: Add the bitter melon and chikuwa and stir-fry over medium heat. Bitter melon retains its nutrients even when well-cooked, so don't worry about overcooking it.
Add the bean sprouts last and stir-fry briefly. Season with soy sauce and mirin.
steps_en.tip: Adding bean sprouts at the very end is grandma's method - keeps them crisp

steps_en.text: Pour in the beaten egg, stir gently, and top with a generous amount of bonito flakes. Serve!
